tEn: Ancient Hindu Weapons: The Brahmastra and Autonomous Arms in the Mahabharata
tZh: 古代印度武器：《摩诃婆罗多》中的梵天法宝与自主武器
sEn: A guest discusses the Mahabharata's description of the Brahmastra as a hydrogen bomb equivalent and other autonomous weapons, suggesting possible lost advanced civilizations.
sZh: 嘉宾讨论《摩诃婆罗多》中将梵天法宝描述为氢弹等价物及其他自主武器，暗示可能存在失落的先进文明。

consensus.en: Curiosity is the most important human quality, driving success and fulfillment.
consensus.zh: 好奇心是最重要的人类品质，驱动成功和满足感。

consensus.en: Ancient texts like the Mahabharata describe advanced weapons resembling modern technology.
consensus.zh: 《摩诃婆罗多》等古代文本描述的先进武器与现代技术相似。

consensus.en: History may involve cycles of advanced civilizations rising and falling.
consensus.zh: 历史可能涉及先进文明的兴衰循环。

consensus.en: AI will replace many jobs, but humans will find new purposes in curiosity and relationships.
consensus.zh: AI 将取代许多工作，但人类会在好奇心和人际关系中找到新目标。

consensus.en: Education should reward asking good questions, not memorizing answers.
consensus.zh: 教育应奖励提出好问题，而非记忆答案。

contrarian.en: The transistor may have been reverse-engineered from crashed UFOs at Bell Labs.
contrarian.zh: 晶体管可能是从贝尔实验室坠毁 UFO 逆向工程而来。

contrarian.en: Ancient structures like pyramids and temples suggest lost advanced technology.
contrarian.zh: 金字塔和寺庙等古代建筑暗示失落的先进技术。

contrarian.en: The concept of a 'knowledge worker' was created by Microsoft to sell software.
contrarian.zh: “知识工作者”概念是微软为销售软件而创造的。

contrarian.en: Social media algorithms curb curiosity and are harmful, especially for kids.
contrarian.zh: 社交媒体算法抑制好奇心，尤其对孩子有害。

contrarian.en: Local AI models on personal hardware can counter big tech's narrative control.
contrarian.zh: 个人硬件上的本地 AI 模型可以对抗大科技公司的叙事控制。

turns.en: How many podcasts have you done?
turns.zh: 你做过多少期播客了？

turns.en: I don't know. I don't know the count, but maybe tens.
turns.zh: 我不知道。我不清楚具体数字，但可能有几十期。

turns.en: Well, when we were talking in the lobby, I was like this good dude would be a good guess because we were talking about ancient Hindu scriptures where you were telling me about something that sounds like a nuclear bomb.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们在大厅聊天时，我就觉得你这家伙肯定猜得准，因为我们当时在聊古印度经文，你跟我讲了一些听起来像核弹的东西。

turns.en: Yeah. The Brahmastra is part of the Mahabharata.
turns.zh: 对。梵天法宝是《摩诃婆罗多》的一部分。

turns.en: I mean you've talked about Mahabharata in a bunch and...
turns.zh: 我是说你聊过很多次《摩诃婆罗多》了……

turns.en: Yeah. So the Mahabharata is one of the two Hindu epics. The other one is Ramayana. But Mahabharata is more interesting. It's more complicated. It's like a lot of different stories interwoven together. And the Brahmastra is the equivalent of the hydrogen bomb.
turns.zh: 对。《摩诃婆罗多》是两大印度史诗之一，另一部是《罗摩衍那》。但《摩诃婆罗多》更有趣，也更复杂，就像许多不同故事交织在一起。而梵天法宝就相当于氢弹。

turns.en: And how is it described?
turns.zh: 它是怎么被描述的？

turns.en: It's described as a weapon of mass destruction that would annihilate the human population. It should not be used at any cost. There's a moral contract. You clearly have to be violating so many things at a deeply moral level to even wield it. And it's not accessible to most warriors. There were probably only two warriors in that era who were allowed to use it. And it has to be passed through special access, like a teacher has to pass on the secret to you, almost like the equivalent of a nuclear code. Arjuna had it. He was a particular character in Mahabharata. He was allowed to use it. And then there was another person: Arjuna had a teacher named Drona, and Drona had a son named Ashwatthama. Ashwatthama was always jealous of Arjuna. Arjuna was not Drona's son but he was his model disciple, so Drona passed on the secret of the Brahmastra to him. Drona's son also wanted it, but because it was his son, he also passed on the secret to his son even though the son wasn't as good as Arjuna. During the war, Arjuna and Ashwatthama fought on opposite sides. The teacher died in the war, so the son got mad and unleashed the Brahmastra. Lord Krishna had to come and save the planet from that destructive force.
turns.zh: 它被描述为一种大规模杀伤性武器，会毁灭人类。无论如何都不能使用。这涉及一种道德契约。要使用它，你必须在深层的道德层面严重违规。大多数战士都无法获得它。那个时代可能只有两位战士被允许使用。它需要通过特殊途径传承，比如老师把秘密传授给你，几乎相当于核密码。阿周那拥有它。他是《摩诃婆罗多》中的一个特定人物，被允许使用。还有另一个人：阿周那有个老师叫德罗纳，德罗纳有个儿子叫阿什瓦塔玛。阿什瓦塔玛一直嫉妒阿周那。阿周那不是德罗纳的儿子，但他是他的模范弟子，所以德罗纳把梵天法宝的秘密传给了他。德罗纳的儿子也想要，但因为是他儿子，德罗纳也把秘密传给了儿子，尽管儿子不如阿周那。战争期间，阿周那和阿什瓦塔玛站在对立面。老师在战争中死了，儿子气疯了，释放了梵天法宝。克利须那神不得不来拯救地球，避免被那股毁灭力量摧毁。

turns.en: How old is the Mahabharata?
turns.zh: 《摩诃婆罗多》有多古老？

turns.en: There are many different opinions, so I don't know for sure. My understanding is it's at least 1,500 to 2,500 years old. 1,500 years ago is the minimum, 2,500 years ago is the maximum. So it happened somewhere in that thousand-year timeframe. And it's still unclear if a lot of it is just mythologized. What actually happened was just a war between kins. There were two groups of people: the Pandavas and the Kauravas. Each side thought they were fighting for their own rights and justice, but at the end of the day, you can crudely understand it as essentially a fight for the kingdom. There was a previous generation with two brothers, and both brothers had a bunch of kids. Those kids were warring to be the next in line, and that ended up being a massive war with many allies fighting on each side. So many amazing weapons were used, described in extreme detail. There's a lot of detail about targeted weapons, so you could precisely identify a target and just shoot at that.
turns.zh: 有很多不同说法，所以我不确定。据我所知，它至少有 1500 到 2500 年历史。1500 年是最低，2500 年是最高。所以它发生在那千年间的某个时期。目前还不清楚其中有多少只是神话化了的。实际发生的只是一场家族间的战争。有两群人：般度族和俱卢族。双方都认为自己在为自己的权利和正义而战，但归根结底，你可以粗略理解为争夺王位。上一代有两兄弟，每人都有一堆孩子。这些孩子为了继承权而争斗，最终演变成一场大战，许多盟友各自参战。使用了那么多惊人的武器，描述极其详细。关于精准武器有很多细节，你可以精确锁定目标并直接攻击。

turns.en: Does it explain what the weapon is?
turns.zh: 它解释武器是什么吗？

turns.en: Yeah. There's one weapon called the Agneyastra where you can specifically target any particular person or group, and it would automatically direct itself and do it, almost like a semi-autonomous weapon. And Lord Krishna had this weapon called the Sudarshana Chakra. It's basically a discus, and you can release it, and it'll go and specifically identify somebody, chop off their head, and come back to you. It self-directs itself. What amazed me is how interesting it is in terms of the autonomy in the weapons—semi-autonomy or autonomy—where the weapons could just be directed at people or a group of soldiers, and it would go and do its job and come back to the wielder. There were so many different astras: Vayunastra, Nagastra, Brahmastra is obviously the ultimate, the hydrogen bomb equivalent. All of these are described in a lot of detail, including who has access to them. Of course, it's mythologized, so it's described as arrows in your back or shoulders, but you could understand it as somebody having access to a lot of weapons. Whoever was powerful would go capture, colonize, and gain power. Essentially, it's a fight between a group of cousins. That's the bottom line of that story.
turns.zh: 有。有一种武器叫火神箭，你可以专门瞄准任何特定的人或群体，它会自动导向并执行，几乎像半自主武器。克利须那神有一种武器叫妙见神轮，基本上是一个飞盘，你释放它，它会去专门识别某人，砍下他的头，然后回到你身边。它自我导向。让我惊叹的是这些武器的自主性——半自主或全自主——武器可以被导向攻击人或一群士兵，然后完成任务回到使用者手中。有那么多不同的法宝：风神箭、蛇神箭，梵天法宝显然是终极的，相当于氢弹。所有这些都有详细描述，包括谁有权使用。当然，这是神话化的，所以被描述为背上的箭，但你可以理解为某人拥有大量武器。谁强大谁就去征服、殖民、获取权力。本质上，这是一群表亲之间的争斗。这就是那个故事的核心。

turns.en: Now, if we think of history as this linear progression from caveman to us, and we hear about autonomous weapons that were written in the Mahabharata somewhere around 2,000 plus years ago, we go, well, a mythology. But if not, if there's been some sort of rise and fall of civilization, if there has been catastrophic events—asteroid impacts, shifting of the poles, whatever it is—it's caused great disasters. You can imagine that these people are remembering a time where there was some sort of very advanced civilization. And this is what they're describing. Like if you knew for a fact that there had been a great advanced technologically advanced civilization when we have evidence that they had some technology like the pyramids of Giza and stuff like how did you do that? I don't know how there's some technology involved, right? But we don't have evidence of the technology. But if we did, if we knew, you would look at the Mahabharata and go, 'Oh, this is history.' They're just explaining it in a kind of crude contemporary way for the time. Arrows instead of, you know, semi-autonomous drones with exploding heads on them. I mean, that's what we have now. All those things that they're describing, hydrogen bomb, semi-autonomous and autonomous drones. I mean, they have autonomous fighter jets now. They don't need people anymore.
turns.zh: 现在，如果我们把历史看作从穴居人到我们的线性进步，然后听到《摩诃婆罗多》中大约 2000 多年前写到的自主武器，我们会说，嗯，神话。但如果不是这样，如果文明有过兴衰，如果有过灾难性事件——小行星撞击、极移等等——造成了巨大灾难。你可以想象这些人是在回忆某个非常先进的文明时代。这就是他们描述的内容。就像如果你确切知道曾经有一个高度先进的科技文明，我们有证据表明他们拥有一些技术，比如吉萨金字塔之类的东西，你是怎么做到的？我不知道其中涉及什么技术，对吧？但我们没有技术的证据。但如果我们有，如果我们知道，你就会看着《摩诃婆罗多》说，‘哦，这是历史。’他们只是用当时粗陋的当代方式解释。用箭代替半自主的爆炸头无人机。我的意思是，我们现在就有这些东西。他们描述的所有东西，氢弹、半自主和自主无人机。我是说，现在已经有自主战斗机了。它们不再需要人了。

transcript.sec: Discussion of ancient epics and weapons

turns.en: Like this we're in that area right now. So when you read about something like that from the Mahabharata, you go like okay what was really going on?
turns.zh: 就像我们现在就在这个领域。所以当你读到《摩诃婆罗多》里那样的东西时，你会想，好吧，到底发生了什么？

turns.en: Exactly. Yeah. I mean that's always been my fascination with those epics and the level of detail with which they described all these weapons and who had access, different levels of access, the status required to have access, and how it was used in the wars, different formations of the soldiers. They had all these crazy formation structures like forming the army like a lotus, forming the army like a chakra vyuha, like literally concentric circles. So you cannot actually get into the innermost circle without going through the outer circles, and you can get killed by each of the flanks whenever you're trying to enter. And the secret of how to actually break into these vyuhas, formations, was only known to a few people. It's incredible. You could say somebody had to be extremely skillful to have that sort of visualization and imagination to describe a story like that. And obviously Tolkien has done an amazing job with a lot of the rings, creating so much detail, and a lot of it actually coming through in real life in some form. Again, not exactly the same weapons but similar style makes you wonder: was there actually something around then? People have tried excavations in all these areas. There are two main areas in the Mahabharata, like Hastinapura was the name of the kingdom, and people have done excavations around there and have found some artifacts that might date back to those years. But there are also some details described in the epics that don't quite align with reality. For example, all the main warriors in that era were described as very tall, very big, 7 or 8 feet, whatever. But studies by archaeologists also say that people who lived in those years in those regions were probably not more than 6 feet tall. So it's not clear exactly what happened, what was correct, what was not correct. We just have to keep probing more. But I find the idea fascinating to think of what could have existed in sacred texts that was only partially communicated to the next generation and having a lot of reinterpretations.
turns.zh: 没错。是的。我一直对这些史诗着迷，它们描述武器、谁有权使用、不同级别的权限、所需地位，以及如何在战争中使用、士兵的不同阵型，细节极其丰富。他们有很多疯狂的阵型结构，比如把军队排成莲花状，排成‘轮阵’，就是同心圆。你无法不经过外层圆圈就进入最内层，而且每次试图进入时都可能被侧翼杀死。而真正破解这些阵型的秘密只有少数人知道。这太不可思议了。可以说，有人必须极其熟练才能有那样的视觉化和想象力来描述这样的故事。显然，托尔金在指环王方面做得非常出色，创造了那么多细节，而且很多在现实中以某种形式呈现。同样，虽然不是完全相同的武器，但风格相似，让人怀疑当时是否真的存在过。人们在这些地区进行过挖掘。摩诃婆罗多中有两个主要地区，比如象城是王国的名字，人们在那里挖掘出了一些可能追溯到那些年代的文物。但史诗中描述的一些细节与现实并不完全吻合。例如，那个时代的所有主要战士都被描述为非常高、非常壮，七八英尺。但考古学家的研究也表明，那些年代生活在那些地区的人可能不超过六英尺。所以不清楚到底发生了什么，哪些正确，哪些不正确。我们只能继续探索。但我发现这个想法很迷人：神圣文本中可能存在的东西，只部分地传给了下一代，并且有很多重新诠释。

turns.en: Another thing that is very interesting to think about is Vedic math. Basically Vedic math is like a branch of mathematics that some people in India grow up learning. I read it myself too, and some people actually practice it just to be sharper at mental math for doing their exams like GMAT and GRE. It has a line in the Vedas that says 'one from the last digit, two from the first digit' whatever, so many different ways of multiplying two different numbers like 97 * 96. Oh, like subtract the last two digits, put it on the right, multiply the first digits, put it on the left. That's the result. And then you wonder, oh wait, the Rigveda is so old. It's the oldest sacred text out there. How is it describing computation? That feels very unreal. Like do they actually know or understand advanced forms of computation even back in those days?
turns.zh: 另一个非常有趣的想法是吠陀数学。基本上，吠陀数学是数学的一个分支，一些印度人从小学习。我自己也读过，有些人实际练习它只是为了在 GMAT 和 GRE 等考试中更擅长心算。吠陀经中有一行说‘末位减一，首位加二’之类的，有很多不同的方法相乘两个不同的数字，比如 97 乘以 96。哦，比如减去最后两位，放在右边，乘以前几位，放在左边。这就是结果。然后你想，等等，《梨俱吠陀》那么古老。它是最古老的神圣文本。它怎么会描述计算？这感觉很不真实。他们当时真的知道或理解高级形式的计算吗？

turns.en: Right.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: And how old is Rigveda?
turns.zh: 《梨俱吠陀》有多古老？

turns.en: I don't exactly know how old it is.
turns.zh: 我不确切知道它有多古老。

turns.en: Why don't we put that in Perplexity?
turns.zh: 我们为什么不查一下 Perplexity？

turns.en: Yeah, let's do that.
turns.zh: 好，我们查一下。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Yeah, it is technically the oldest sacred text out there.
turns.zh: 是的，严格来说它是最古老的神圣文本。

turns.en: And so what's interesting is I wonder how old the stories were by the time they were written down. Like how much of it is relayed person to person for years and years just like the Bible before it's ever actually written down. Scholars usually date the composition of the Rigveda to about 1500 to 1200 BCE. So its oldest layer is roughly 3,200 to 3,700 years old today.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，我想知道这些故事在被写下来时有多古老。就像圣经一样，在真正写下来之前，有多少是年复一年口口相传的。学者们通常将《梨俱吠陀》的创作年代定在公元前 1500 年到 1200 年左右。所以它最古老的层今天大约有 3200 到 3700 年历史。

turns.en: Like every ancient culture has a story of a flood. Every one, they all have an apocalyptic story.
turns.zh: 就像每个古老文化都有一个大洪水的故事。每一个，都有末日故事。

turns.en: Mahabharata had the same thing.
turns.zh: 《摩诃婆罗多》也有同样的故事。

turns.en: Was it?
turns.zh: 是吗？

turns.en: Mahabharata had the same thing where there was a big almost like a tsunami thing. I don't exactly know what it was called, but that was the collapse of Lord Krishna's kingdom Dwaraka after the war. A lot of people died, but some people survived, and even those who survived got wiped out by a calamity or some kind of fight among themselves. Most of the people who participated in that era actually died.
turns.zh: 《摩诃婆罗多》也有类似的事情，有一个几乎像海啸一样的东西。我不确切知道它叫什么，但那是战后克里希纳神的王国德瓦拉卡崩溃。很多人死了，但有些人幸存下来，甚至那些幸存者也被一场灾难或他们之间的某种争斗消灭了。那个时代的大多数参与者实际上都死了。

turns.en: Here it is. The primordial, how do you say it? Manu.
turns.zh: 找到了。原始的，怎么说？摩奴。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Manu flood. Classic Hindu great flood myth where the righteous king Manu is warned by a divine fish about an imminent deluge that will destroy humanity. He builds a boat, loads it with his family. It's like Noah's ark. The same thing with seeds and animals. Ties it to the horn of the god in fish form which tows the boat to safety until the waters recede and the world is repopulated.
turns.zh: 摩奴洪水。经典的印度教大洪水神话，正义的国王摩奴被一条神鱼警告即将有一场毁灭人类的洪水。他造了一艘船，装上他的家人。就像诺亚方舟。同样带着种子和动物。把船系在鱼形神的角上，神把船拖到安全的地方，直到洪水退去，世界重新繁衍生息。

turns.en: They all have the same story. Yeah. That's what's really crazy.
turns.zh: 他们都有同样的故事。是的。这真的很疯狂。

turns.en: There is a concept in Hindu philosophy called the yugas.
turns.zh: 印度教哲学中有一个概念叫‘由迦’（时代）。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: I'm reading a book about it right now.
turns.zh: 我现在正在读一本关于它的书。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: So there are different yugas and yugas are like thousands of years, and the concept is that the yugas keep cycling around. So we are in the Kali Yuga right now, and before that was the Dwapara Yuga, that's when most of Mahabharata happened, and before that there was a Treta Yuga where the Ramayana happened, and before that there was another yuga.
turns.zh: 所以有不同的由迦，由迦就像几千年，概念是由迦不断循环。我们现在处于‘迦利由迦’（黑暗时代），之前是‘德瓦帕拉由迦’，那是《摩诃婆罗多》大部分发生的时候，再之前是‘特雷塔由迦’，那是《罗摩衍那》发生的时候，再之前还有另一个由迦。

turns.en: What is next after Kali Yuga?
turns.zh: 迦利由迦之后是什么？

turns.en: No, there is nothing next after Kali Yuga. It goes back to the first one. I forget the name of the first yuga.
turns.zh: 不，迦利由迦之后没有别的。它回到第一个。我忘了第一个由迦的名字。

turns.en: Because the interpretation that I'm reading is that we're not in Kali Yuga anymore and that Kali Yuga ended in the 1900s and Dwapara Yuga started then.
turns.zh: 因为我读到的解释是，我们不再处于迦利由迦，迦利由迦在 1900 年代结束，德瓦帕拉由迦那时开始了。

turns.en: No, no, we are in Kali Yuga right now.
turns.zh: 不，不，我们现在处于迦利由迦。
百分之百。

turns.zh: 我们可以寻求大量信息，获取信息的速度比以往任何时候都快。感觉那是唯一一种普遍的人类品质，从古代、从最古老的文本就存在了。事实上，在《梨俱吠陀》中，明确鼓励你寻求智慧胜过财富。这不仅仅是印度教特有的想法。这个特定的想法存在于《圣经》中。存在于《古兰经》中。存在于《托拉》中。并不是说宗教文本告诫人们追求财富。实际上是寻求智慧更重要。嗯，你知道，这就是为什么我说你是一个很好的例子，当然你有一个非常大的播客，但你运营它的方式就是你对很多事情感到好奇，问很多问题，我认为那是非常重要的一个品质。所以嗯，我觉得这是最古老的东西，是我们从古代就知道的唯一的东西——保持好奇。

turns.en: Well I I I think it's stimulating to people and genuine curiosity is stimulating to other people. when when someone is genuinely curious about something, I become curious about it. I think it's contagious.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为这对人们有激励作用，真诚的好奇心会激励其他人。当某人对某件事真正好奇时，我也会对它好奇。我认为这是有感染力的。

turns.en: And I think that it's it's also an authentic quality. And I think there's there's something about really wanting to know something and being interested in something. And if you're curious, generally, you're going to ask more questions about something so you have a deeper understanding of it. So if you're trying to do whatever you're trying to do, a sport, a game, you you'll probably get better at it because you're more curious because instead of just assuming things, you'll ask more questions. You'll reexamine things.
turns.zh: 而且我认为这也是一种真实的品质。我认为真正想知道某件事、对某件事感兴趣，这里面有某种东西。如果你好奇，通常你会问更多问题，从而对它有更深的理解。所以如果你在尝试做任何你想做的事，一项运动，一个游戏，你可能会做得更好，因为你更好奇，因为你不是仅仅假设，而是会问更多问题。你会重新审视事物。

transcript.sec: Curiosity and the origin of the podcast

turns.en: It's one of the most important human qualities. And to me, it's one of the most attractive human qualities. It's always been. When I meet curious people, I'm always interested. I'm always like, 'Tell me what you're curious about.' And I'll tell you what I'm curious about. Let's talk. And this podcast started out genuinely because of a lot of just talking with friends, but it also led into one of my very first actual guests, Graham Hancock. And it's just because I was curious because I had read Fingerprints of the Gods and I'd seen him talk. I'd seen speeches and I'm like, I want to know what you know, what you think's going on. And he's another guy incredibly curious and absolutely fascinated with his takes on ancient history. He has been talking about this subject a long time. When he first wrote Fingerprints of the Gods, I think that came out in like 97 or 98 or something like that. And I remember reading it and so many of my educated friends said this is bullshit, why are you paying attention to this? But more and more as time goes on, it's been proven that he's correct. The timelines shifted back. From the publication of that book, the discovery of Gobekli Tepe and the surrounding area, like it's like, okay, now we realize there was some crazy going on at the very least 11,000 years ago. So we pushed civilization back 5,000 years. And this is just what we found now. And we keep finding things. Keep digging. Keep looking.
turns.zh: 这是人类最重要的品质之一。对我来说，它也是最吸引人的品质之一，一直如此。当我遇到充满好奇心的人，我总是很感兴趣。我总会说，'告诉我你对什么好奇。'我也会告诉你我对什么好奇。我们聊聊吧。这个播客最初确实是因为和朋友聊天开始的，但也引出了我最早的正式嘉宾之一，格雷厄姆·汉考克。这只是因为我很想知道他知道什么，他认为发生了什么。他也是一个极其好奇的人，对古代历史的见解非常着迷。他谈论这个话题已经很久了。他第一次写《上帝的指纹》时，我想大概是 97 或 98 年。我记得读过那本书，我很多受过教育的朋友都说这是胡说八道，你为什么关注这个？但随着时间的推移，越来越多的事实证明他是对的。时间线被推后了。从那本书出版以来，哥贝克力石阵及其周边地区的发现，让我们意识到至少在一万一千年前就有一些疯狂的事情发生。所以我们将文明推后了五千年。而这只是我们目前发现的。我们还在不断发现新东西。继续挖掘，继续寻找。

transcript.sec: Under the pyramids: radar tomography discoveries

turns.en: And then you see the stuff that they're finding underneath the pyramid with this radio tomography where they're looking under the pyramid and it seems that there are structures under the pyramid. You've seen that stuff.
turns.zh: 然后你看到他们用无线电断层扫描在金字塔下面发现的东西，他们正在查看金字塔下方，似乎下面有结构。你看到过那些东西。

turns.en: I haven't seen that.
turns.zh: 我没看到过。

turns.en: I had the scientist that's involved in it. He's an Italian guy, Filippo Bondi, and he came on the podcast. Wonderful accent. Almost as good as yours. It was amazing. But he's describing the use of this stuff and that they've used it successfully on known areas in pyramids and other structures and they can detect, in fact, there's in Italy a particle collider that is underneath a mountain. And using this technology, which is satellite-based technology, they get an accurate description of this particle collider that's I think it's 1,200 meters underground. Like how far is that thing underground? We'll find out. But it's like deep under stone. And they find that they can get an accurate depiction, they can actually give you the dimensions of this particle collider. They have like an image of it. And this same technology is showing that there are these columns underneath the pyramid in various places that are 20 meters wide and they have coils around them. They don't know what the hell they are. And the whole structure of this thing, it's not small. It goes almost a kilometer into the ground. There's like this enormous bottom of it. And it seems like it's something that's constructed. And so they're like, 'Okay, well, the pyramid is crazy. It's crazy enough, but if there's something underneath it that's man-made or someone made it that's a kilometer deep into the ground, like what the hell are we even talking about?' Like, who made this? What did they have?
turns.zh: 我请来了参与其中的科学家。他是个意大利人，菲利波·邦迪，他上了播客。口音很棒，几乎和你的口音一样好。太棒了。但他描述了这项技术的使用，他们已经在金字塔和其他结构的已知区域成功应用了它。事实上，在意大利有一个粒子对撞机位于山底下。利用这项基于卫星的技术，他们得到了这个粒子对撞机的精确描述，它位于地下 1200 米深处。那东西在地下有多深？我们会知道的。但它深埋在石头下面。他们发现可以获得精确的图像，实际上可以给出这个粒子对撞机的尺寸。他们有一张它的图像。同样的技术显示，金字塔下方有多个地方存在这些柱子，宽 20 米，周围有线圈。他们不知道那到底是什么。整个结构不小，它深入地下近一公里。有一个巨大的底部。看起来像是人造的。所以他们想，'好吧，金字塔已经很疯狂了，但如果它下面还有人造的东西，深入地下近一公里，那我们到底在说什么？'是谁建造的？他们有什么技术？

turns.en: 1.2 km in?
turns.zh: 1.2 公里深？

turns.en: 1.2 kilometers into the mountain. That's nuts. It's half a mile into the mountain. And this thing can see through all that and get this accurate depiction of this particle collider. And it's showing with multiple scans, not just one, multiple scans and different technology, the same exact images, the same exact structures underneath this immense 2,300,000 stone structure that almost perfectly aligns to true north, south, east, and west. Like what? What was going on? Don't tell me copper tools. Like what the hell was going on? Something crazy. And I have a feeling our simplistic explanation of it is just doing no one any justice. It's doing no service to history. It's doing no service to our understanding. They've got to be a little bit more open in the fact that they are perplexed.
turns.zh: 深入山体 1.2 公里。太疯狂了。深入山体半英里。这个东西可以看穿所有这些，得到这个粒子对撞机的精确描绘。而且通过多次扫描，不止一次，多次扫描和不同的技术，显示完全相同的图像，完全相同的结构，就在这个巨大的 230 万块石头结构下方，它几乎完美地指向正北、正南、正东、正西。这到底是怎么回事？别跟我说铜工具。到底发生了什么？太疯狂了。我觉得我们过于简单的解释对任何人都没有好处。对历史没有帮助，对我们的理解也没有帮助。他们必须更坦率地承认他们感到困惑。

transcript.secZh: 金字塔下：雷达断层扫描发现

turns.en: And not just perplexed by stuff like this. This is a 3D print of an actual vase that exists in Egypt that they found in tombs of the old kingdom. This thing was somehow made with diorite, so it's incredibly hard stone, and made to like a thousandth of a human hair in precision. It's crazy dimensions. The precision of it and it wasn't turned on a lathe because it has handles. So you look at the handles on the side. Well, you can't carve those and those are perfect too. The alignment of everything and it's like you just look at it. Oh, it's a vase. No big deal. But no, it's kind of crazy. Like how did they cut that out? There are also all these core marks in some of the stones that they find in Egypt and they've analyzed the amount of revolutions per minute that you would have to go through to be able to cut through something and leave these lines and it defies explanation. Like what is this? This is crazy. This is not sand and copper and just rubbing things. No, this is some insane technology that we don't understand. There are scoop marks out of the bottoms of some of these stones. It's like what? What the hell is this? How'd you scoop rock? Like what? It looks like ice cream. Like they just went like what are they doing there? There are so many questions.
turns.zh: 而且不仅仅是困惑于这类东西。这是一个实际存在的埃及花瓶的 3D 打印品，发现于古王国时期的墓葬中。这个东西是用闪长岩制成的，这是一种极其坚硬的石头，精度达到了人类头发的千分之一。尺寸非常疯狂。它的精度，而且它不是用车床加工的，因为它有把手。所以你看侧面的把手。嗯，你无法雕刻它们，而且它们也很完美。所有东西的对齐，你看着它，哦，只是一个花瓶。没什么大不了的。但不对，这有点疯狂。他们是怎么切割出来的？在埃及发现的一些石头上也有所有这些核心痕迹，他们分析了每分钟所需的转数才能切割出这样的线条，这无法解释。这是什么？太疯狂了。这不是沙子和铜，只是摩擦。不，这是某种我们不了解的疯狂技术。有些石头的底部有勺状痕迹。这到底是什么？你怎么挖石头？看起来像冰淇淋。他们就像那样，他们在做什么？有太多问题了。

turns.en: What tools did they even have to do all these things?
turns.zh: 他们到底有什么工具来做所有这些事情？

turns.en: They had copper. I mean there's some evidence that they had some iron and then I think Tutankhamun had a dagger that was actually made from meteorite which is interesting. You know, when they could find meteorites and make things out of them, it was very valuable, obviously. But the sheer volume of work that they did there, if you look at the temple in Karnak, you look at all the three major pyramids, you look at all the different temples and all the construction, and the older you go, the deeper into the sand they go, the more complex these things are, which is even weirder.
turns.zh: 他们有铜。我的意思是，有一些证据表明他们有一些铁，然后我想图坦卡蒙有一把匕首实际上是用陨石制成的，这很有趣。你知道，当他们能找到陨石并用它们制作东西时，显然非常有价值。但他们在那里所做的工作量之大，如果你看看卡纳克神庙，看看三大金字塔，看看所有不同的寺庙和所有建筑，而且越往古代，越深入沙土，这些东西就越复杂，这甚至更奇怪。

turns.en: So it seems like civilization after civilization just they would there was probably a rise and fall with their technology as well.
turns.zh: 所以似乎一个又一个文明，他们的技术可能也有兴衰。

turns.en: Absolutely. I think it's just incredible that none of this knowledge was properly documented ever. And it's a whole line of work to just go understand like how to even rebuild these things, let alone how did they build it.
turns.zh: 绝对。我认为令人难以置信的是，这些知识从未被妥善记录。要理解如何重建这些东西本身就是一项工作，更不用说他们是如何建造的了。

turns.en: Well think about what we're doing right now. All of our knowledge is essentially stored on hard drives and paper.
turns.zh: 嗯，想想我们现在在做什么。我们所有的知识基本上都存储在硬盘和纸上。

transcript.secZh: 古代文物：超乎解释的精度

turns.en: Those are the two things that are going to deteriorate the quickest. Maybe we should take a dump of the internet and put it on a rock, go preserve it somewhere so that even if our civilization is wiped out and all the data centers are gone, whoever comes next can figure it out.
turns.zh: 这两样东西会最快退化。也许我们应该把互联网的数据转存到石头上，保存起来，这样即使我们的文明被摧毁，所有数据中心都消失了，后来的人也能搞清楚。

turns.en: Well, you've got to assume that even if they found a hard drive, how long would it take for them to reverse-engineer what we did and figure out what these ones and zeros actually mean?
turns.zh: 嗯，你得假设即使他们找到了硬盘，要花多长时间才能逆向工程我们做过的事，弄明白这些 0 和 1 到底是什么意思？

turns.en: Yeah. That is one of the most bizarre and fantastic accomplishments of modern civilization: this is a terabyte. Which is nuts.
turns.zh: 是啊。这是现代文明最离奇、最了不起的成就之一：这是一个太字节。太疯狂了。

turns.en: I remember the old Android phones. You get an SD card, you could slip one in there and store images on that to save space. All that stuff is so vulnerable. Linux? It would be a nightmare.
turns.zh: 我记得老安卓手机。你可以插一张 SD 卡，把图片存上去省空间。所有这些都太脆弱了。如果一个完全陌生的外星文明降临，找到我们的硬盘，而他们走了完全不同的技术路线，他们就得逆向工程我们做的一切，试图弄清楚我们用的什么操作系统，怎么工作的——是 Unix？Linux？那将是一场噩梦。

turns.en: They would need an advanced AI to figure it all out for them, right?
turns.zh: 他们需要一个先进的人工智能来帮他们搞清楚一切，对吧？

Then you start reading in the Rig Veda about stuff that happened thousands of years ago and you go, 'Wait, what the hell is this?'
turns.zh: 从宏观角度看，那并不算长。但对人类来说，相当长。想想我们花了多久才步入正轨。经历了成千上万年，人们可能像野兽一样，是最坏的那种。那可能是他们生存的唯一方式。可能有很多同类相食、很多谋杀，5000 年里发生了很多可怕的事情，直到人们慢慢重新学会农业。开始建墙，大家都放松了一点，有了可靠的武器来驱赶别人，这样你才能研究数学。然后转眼间，文明再次出现，又回到了循环中。然后你开始读《梨俱吠陀》里关于几千年前发生的事情，你会想：‘等等，这他妈是什么？’

turns.zh: 是的，如果能选择回到过去的一个时间窗口看看，我百分之百会选择古埃及建造金字塔的时候。让我看看当时到底发生了什么。只要把我放进一个大仓鼠轮，一个没人能看到我的大塑料泡泡里。让我违反时空，在那里存在几分钟。就让我看看。我认为那是人类历史上你能看到的最疯狂的事情。

turns.en: Yeah. I just want to know what they knew, what they had, what they used. Look, Petra is same time period, at least attributed to 7,000 roughly BC. Jesus. And how would you do that?
