turns.zh: 五年前，那些在 AI 领域最顶尖的人——我不认为自己属于那个层次——但五年前在 AI 决策最前沿的人，我不认为他们预测到了我们今天所处的确切状态。没有人预测到。如果他们预测到了，他们早就已经获取了所有算力，制造了所有芯片，买下了所有晶圆厂。他们肯定会这么做，对吧？这个反事实：每个人都因为算力不足而受制。我们没有足够的芯片，没有足够的电力。这些都是问题。如果你邀请任何 AI 领域的人，问他们今天 AI 的瓶颈是什么，每个人都会说是电力。我想 Jensen 在这里时也说了同样的话，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: But if you predicted this exact state five years before, wouldn't you have secured enough power and started building more power plants yourself, started getting permits, started planning out capacity? No. Nobody did that.
turns.zh: 但如果你五年前就预测到了这种状态，难道你不会确保足够的电力，自己开始建造更多发电厂，开始获取许可，开始规划容量吗？不。没有人那样做。

turns.en: No. Everything is reactive to the demand that we're having today.
turns.zh: 没有。一切都是对今天需求的反应。

turns.en: And that's just five years. So when you ask me to predict 250 years, I just have to honestly say I don't know.
turns.zh: 而这只是五年。所以当你让我预测 250 年时，我只能老实说我不知道。

turns.en: Do you ever sit back and think about it though? What it could be?
turns.zh: 但你有没有静下来思考过？它可能会是什么样子？

turns.en: I do think about it. There are a lot of fun things. I use Perplexity a lot for these kinds of things, especially this new feature, Computer Use inside it. And one hypothetical scenario: let's say there is an AGI, right? And I've seen you ask a lot of people about this, and a lot of conventional answers are like, 'Oh, we'll just become managers of the AIs, don't worry.' But if the price of cognition is the price of compute, managing an AI is also pretty much doable by the AI itself because the bottleneck is not unique cognitive capability there. So the value of society will automatically shift to what is scarce. And fundamentally, what has been scarce is asking high-quality questions about things. What if we just completely spend all our time understanding the past? That's an interesting endeavor. It was not cool before, but it'll become cool again. We usually used to view archaeology or history as not something worth having a career in because it doesn't pay well. But what if it actually starts paying you a lot more now that actual knowledge work is being done by AIs and it's all mundane and the price of that is basically at zero?
turns.zh: 我确实思考过。有很多有趣的事情。我经常用 Perplexity 来做这类事情，尤其是它的新功能 Computer Use。有一个假设场景：假设存在一个 AGI，对吧？我看到你问过很多人这个问题，很多常规回答是，‘哦，我们只会成为 AI 的管理者，别担心。’但如果认知的价格就是算力的价格，那么管理 AI 本身也基本可以由 AI 完成，因为瓶颈不在于独特的认知能力。所以社会的价值会自动转向稀缺的东西。而从根本上说，一直稀缺的是提出高质量问题的能力。如果我们把所有时间都用来理解过去呢？那是一项有趣的努力。以前这并不酷，但会重新变得酷起来。我们通常认为考古学或历史学不值得作为职业，因为收入不高。但如果它实际上开始给你带来更多收入呢？因为现在实际的知识工作都由 AI 完成，而且都很平凡，其价格基本为零。

turns.en: Right? And archaeology would be one of the few things that it wouldn't have access to because it doesn't have the actual ground. It can't get into the ground and do the scans.
turns.zh: 对吧？而考古学将是少数 AI 无法触及的领域之一，因为它没有实际的地面。它无法进入地下进行扫描。

turns.en: No, let's say we have robots to go do that.
turns.zh: 不，假设我们有机器人去做那些。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: But you're still going to be the one probing, right? Because you have incomplete information all the time. Even the idea of 'let's go explore this particular area, let's go understand better, or let's go try to reverse engineer that, let's go try to build this again, oh how would it be if we wanted to do the same thing on the moon?' There are so many interesting projects to work on for us, as long as we stay curious and stay interested in a lot of things that we've done before and trying to understand civilization. I'm not really concerned about what things we get to do. We might be doing a lot more cool things for what it's worth. I don't know if anybody will be coming and telling you that it's so cool to open an Excel sheet every day and make financial models, right?
turns.zh: 但你仍然是那个进行探索的人，对吧？因为你总是拥有不完整的信息。即使是‘我们去探索这个特定区域，去更好地理解，或者去逆向工程那个，再去建造这个，如果我们想在月球上做同样的事情会怎样？’这样的想法，也有太多有趣的项目等着我们去做，只要我们保持好奇，对我们以前做过的很多事情保持兴趣，并试图理解文明。我并不担心我们能做什么事情。我们可能会做更多很酷的事情。我不知道是否有人会跑来告诉你，每天打开 Excel 表格做财务模型很酷，对吧？

turns.en: There's got to be somebody out there that likes that.
turns.zh: 肯定有人喜欢那样。

turns.en: I mean, there's something about the task you do and what you get paid for, what is the job title, etc. And some people associate their personal worth with where they work and how much they get paid. And I think that thing is going to collapse in a world where the price of all that cognition is going to be the price of compute.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你所做的任务、你得到的报酬、你的职位头衔等等，这些东西有些意义。有些人将个人价值与他们在哪里工作以及赚多少钱联系起来。我认为在一个所有认知的价格都等于算力价格的世界里，这种东西会崩溃。

transcript.sec: Impact of AI on jobs and society

turns.en: What do you think happens to people if a large percentage of jobs get replaced by AI?
turns.zh: 如果很大比例的工作被 AI 取代，你认为人们会怎样？

turns.en: I think they'll find new things. We've always gravitated towards things that are scarce because that's where the value lies. One interesting analogy is the Gulf States, where there's an abundance of resources and they export their resources to other states, and that pays for the whole state. They offer everybody free electricity, subsidized health, subsidized education, and no taxes. When I first went to Dubai almost 20 years ago, they told me people don't pay taxes here, nobody pays for electricity, and education is super cheap. I was like, 'Wait, how is that real?' And the way that's real is that because the government provides you all these things, it becomes a frontier state. You offer political acquisitions to the state, and what ended up happening is citizens there expect the state to find them jobs, expect the state to take care of job displacement for them, so they don't worry. So it made them a little more lazy. That's not a good future to have where some people talk about AI subsidies and AI dividends that get paid to everybody. I think we need to do some form of that, but that in entirety won't solve the problem.
turns.zh: 我认为他们会找到新的事情做。我们总是倾向于稀缺的东西，因为那里有价值。一个有趣的类比是海湾国家，那里资源丰富，他们将资源出口到其他国家，这支撑了整个国家。他们为所有人提供免费电力、补贴医疗、补贴教育，并且没有税收。当我大约 20 年前第一次去迪拜时，他们告诉我这里的人不纳税，没有人付电费，教育超级便宜。我当时想，‘等等，这怎么可能？’而之所以可能，是因为政府提供了所有这些，它成了一个前沿国家。你向国家提供政治收购，结果公民期望国家为他们找到工作，期望国家为他们处理失业问题，所以他们不担心。这让他们变得有点懒惰。这不是一个好的未来，有些人谈论 AI 补贴和 AI 红利，支付给每个人。我认为我们需要某种形式，但完全依靠它解决不了问题。

turns.en: Well, the thing about human nature is sort of undeniable. And if you give people the ability to be lazy, a large percentage of people will take that.
turns.zh: 嗯，人性这一点是无可否认的。如果你给人们懒惰的能力，很大一部分人会选择懒惰。

turns.en: That's right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: A large percentage won't though.
turns.zh: 但很大一部分人不会。

turns.en: Yeah. There's going to be enough people that are inspired to do something and they say, 'Okay, well now my basic needs are taken care of. Let me pursue my actual interest and find purpose in that.' Because a lot of people find purpose in whatever their occupation is.
turns.zh: 是的。会有足够多的人受到启发去做一些事情，他们会说，‘好吧，现在我的基本需求得到了满足。让我追求我真正的兴趣，并从中找到目标。’因为很多人从他们的职业中找到目标。

turns.en: And if we can shift that to finding purpose in what your actual interests are and then really pursuing something, whatever it is in that, then you'll still have meaning in your life.
turns.zh: 如果我们能将其转变为从你真正的兴趣中找到目标，然后真正去追求某件事，无论是什么，那么你的生活仍然会有意义。

turns.en: And we keep coming back to staying curious. And finding value in your relationships, your family, caring for each other. If you ask a lot of retired people, actually retired people is a good demographic to understand what would happen, what are things people find meaning in after work's taken off them.
turns.zh: 我们一直回到保持好奇心这一点。以及在你的人际关系、家庭、相互关心中找到价值。如果你问很多退休的人，实际上退休人群是了解会发生什么的好样本，了解当工作被剥离后人们从什么中找到意义。

transcript.secZh: AI 对就业与社会的影响

turns.en: And all majority of the answers are always like family, caring, personal relationships and community. These are the things retired people keep doing to keep themselves active and wake up every day and have something to look forward to. So all those things will become even more important at a time when work itself doesn't mean much.
turns.zh: 而且绝大多数答案总是关于家庭、关爱、个人关系和社区。这些都是退休的人为了保持活跃、每天醒来有盼头而持续做的事情。所以当工作本身不再重要时，这些东西会变得更加重要。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Doesn't mean humans won't be status seeking. I think we'll still be, but status is not going to come from whether you're working at a particular famous bank or a tech company or whatever. It'll be driven by how interesting you are. Are you interesting to talk to? When I can talk to an AI like that, are you still interesting to talk to? Are there certain things I get out of talking to you that completely change my perspective about a bunch of things, or is it just fun to hang around you? Can we have a compounding relationship together? And I think again it goes back to being curious about things.
turns.zh: 但这并不意味着人类不会追求地位。我认为我们仍然会，但地位不再来自于你是否在一家著名的银行或科技公司工作。它将取决于你有多有趣。和你聊天有趣吗？当我可以和这样的人工智能聊天时，你仍然有趣吗？和你交谈是否能让我在某些事情上完全改变看法，还是只是和你在一起很开心？我们能否建立一种不断深化的关系？我认为这又回到了对事物保持好奇。

turns.en: Well, this is best case scenario, right? Worst case scenario is civilization upheaval, chaos, civil war, and it's possible. It's possible even without an AI, right?
turns.zh: 嗯，这是最好的情况，对吧？最坏的情况是文明动荡、混乱、内战，而且这是可能的。即使没有人工智能也是可能的，对吧？

turns.en: Exactly.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Look, we've gotten real close to it a couple of times.
turns.zh: 你看，我们已经好几次非常接近这种情况了。

turns.en: Exactly.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Yeah. So we did not need an AGI scenario for a civilizational collapse in the past, as you've clearly seen, right? A calamity can just take out all of us, wipe out everything.
turns.zh: 是的。所以过去我们并不需要 AGI（通用人工智能）的场景就能导致文明崩溃，正如你清楚看到的，对吧？一场灾难就能消灭我们所有人，摧毁一切。

turns.en: Sure. Especially natural ones.
turns.zh: 当然，尤其是自然灾害。

turns.en: Yeah. That's why I'm not a big fan of everybody claiming that AI is going to kill us or that AGI is going to destroy humanity and it's too dangerous and we all need to stop doing these things, but at the same time continuing to build data centers and continuing to make money. You have to have one consistent position. My position is that whether AI or not, I think being curious is going to serve you really well. I think it's going to help you have a better life. And there are two paths to curiosity. One that can kill it and one that can supercharge it. In my opinion, the one that kills curiosity is algorithmic feeds. The brain rot that you're fed every day with just continuous doom scrolling. That's bad. And the one that can supercharge it is AI. Now you can just ask whatever you want if everybody has like a pull-it-up Jamie for them, right? And that's amazing. So all you have to do is be curious about a lot of different things and of course talk to interesting people, engage in interesting activities together. If money is no longer an issue, you can fund passion projects yourself. You don't have to require government funding or venture funding. Like what if you just wanted to build a mini cave yourself? You find a piece of land somewhere. There's a lot of land in America. Way more land than we know what to do with. And surely we can build a lot of interesting things there.
turns.zh: 是的。这就是为什么我不太喜欢每个人都声称人工智能会杀死我们，或者 AGI（通用人工智能）会毁灭人类，太危险了，我们都必须停止这些事情，但同时又在继续建造数据中心和赚钱。你必须有一个一致的立场。我的立场是，无论有没有人工智能，我认为保持好奇会对你非常有益。我认为它会帮助你过上更好的生活。好奇心有两条路：一条会扼杀它，另一条会增强它。在我看来，扼杀好奇心的是算法推送。每天给你灌输的那些脑腐内容，就是无休止的末日滚动。那很糟糕。而能增强好奇心的是人工智能。现在你可以随便问任何问题，如果每个人都有一个像“拉出来杰米”那样的助手，对吧？那太棒了。所以你只需要对很多不同的事情保持好奇，当然还要和有趣的人交谈，一起参与有趣的活动。如果钱不再是问题，你可以自己资助激情项目。你不需要政府资助或风险投资。比如，如果你只是想自己建一个小洞穴呢？你找一块地。美国有很多土地。比我们知道怎么用的多得多。而且我们肯定可以在那里建造很多有趣的东西。

turns.en: Well, that's a good glass half full scenario. And one of the things that I keep coming to is this whole idea of people working and making money and having careers and having portfolios and bank accounts and all. This is all very recent in human history. Very recent. But we've become accustomed to this as a way of life. And Microsoft built this concept of a knowledge worker because they wanted to sell more office software. Really? Yeah. Like this whole idea of putting a PC on every desk and making you glued to the PC was Bill Gates' vision: put a PC on every desk. That wizard. What an incredible accomplishment, because boy did they nail it. Yeah. So it was not about making computing beautiful or anything in the way Steve Jobs envisioned it, right? It was just about selling more software, selling more computers, because that way you can sell more software, and if you sell more software, you become rich. The company was essentially a large sales machine built to sell software. And now they sell cloud, but whatever. That's essentially the reason that we all got trained to use software. People went and did tutorials on how to use Excel, how to use Word, how to use all these email tools, and then that became the upskilling you needed to go work at different companies and write code and whatever. So if that part is going to be done by an AI, it's not necessarily a bad thing, because this is not actually the way you feel real purpose and fulfillment in your own life. If you were never exposed to that, whatever you had as the intrinsic curiosity in you, that's probably what you should be doing.
turns.zh: 嗯，这是一个很好的半杯满的场景。我一直在思考的一个问题是，人们工作、赚钱、拥有职业、投资组合和银行账户等等，这一切在人类历史上都是非常近期的事情。非常近期。但我们已经习惯了这种生活方式。而微软之所以提出知识工作者这个概念，是因为他们想卖更多的办公软件。真的吗？是的。比如，把个人电脑放在每张桌子上，让你粘在电脑上，这是比尔·盖茨的愿景：每张桌子上都有一台个人电脑。那个巫师。多么了不起的成就，因为他们确实做到了。是的。所以这并不像史蒂夫·乔布斯设想的那样让计算变得美丽，对吧？只是为了卖更多的软件，卖更多的电脑，因为这样你就能卖更多的软件，如果你卖更多的软件，你就会变得富有。这家公司本质上是一个巨大的销售机器，用来销售软件。现在他们卖云服务，但不管怎样。这基本上就是我们所有人都被训练使用软件的原因。人们去学习如何使用 Excel、Word、所有电子邮件工具，然后这成为你在不同公司工作、编写代码等所需技能提升的内容。所以如果这部分工作由人工智能完成，这未必是坏事，因为这并不是你真正感受到人生目标和成就感的方式。如果你从未接触过这些，你内在的好奇心可能才是你应该追求的。

turns.en: Yeah. There could be a completely new way to live life where we're not dependent upon labor for basic needs, but then it's going to be incumbent upon people. They're going to have to figure out a way to be either self-starting or we're going to have to expose people to things that excite their curiosity and make that a mandate.
turns.zh: 是的。可能会有一种全新的生活方式，我们不再依赖劳动来满足基本需求，但这将取决于人们自己。他们必须想办法要么自我驱动，要么我们必须让人们接触到能激发他们好奇心的事物，并将其作为一项任务。

turns.en: Yeah. It has to start from schools.
turns.zh: 是的。必须从学校开始。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And as long as we keep rewarding people for having answers instead of asking interesting questions, it's going to be a difficult change. In schools, you're always rewarded for being smart based on whether you have answers to 20 different questions. Who cares? All those 20 questions can be answered by AIs. Have you ever flipped the script where you say, 'Okay, the smartest person in the room is the one who asks the most interesting questions.' What kind of students can you cultivate based on that? Imagine if the room had no pressure to always know the answer, but the freedom to ask a lot of questions. Because sometimes when someone asks a question, it'll make you pause and go, 'I never even thought of that, but that's it. That's the question.' And it takes so many people with so many different perspectives, which is one of the more interesting things I've experienced doing this podcast: I get to talk to so many different people and they vary so widely.
turns.zh: 而且只要我们继续奖励那些有答案的人，而不是提出有趣问题的人，改变就会很困难。在学校里，你总是因为能回答 20 个不同的问题而被认为聪明。谁在乎呢？那 20 个问题人工智能都能回答。你有没有翻转剧本，说：‘好吧，房间里最聪明的人是提出最有趣问题的人。’基于此你能培养出什么样的学生？想象一下，如果房间里没有总是知道答案的压力，而是有提出大量问题的自由。因为有时当有人提出一个问题时，它会让你停下来想：‘我从未想过这一点，但就是这样。这就是问题所在。’这需要很多拥有不同视角的人，这是我做这个播客经历的最有趣的事情之一：我能和这么多不同的人交谈，他们差异巨大。

transcript.sec: The Joy of Curiosity and the Scientific Mindset

turns.en: There's so many different ways of looking at the world and so many different ways of engaging with the world and so many different things that people are fascinated with that they spent their entire life studying and pursuing. It's like you get this rich tapestry of the human experience that I would have never been exposed to this many people.
turns.zh: 看待世界的方式有那么多，参与世界的方式有那么多，人们着迷的东西有那么多，他们一生都在研究和追求。就像你得到了人类经验的丰富画卷，我本来永远不会接触到这么多人。

turns.en: And in turn I've been able to expose these people to all these other folks that are just listening and watching right now. And it's incredible. And it's such a for me it's like the perfect job. I've never had a job that more aligns with my own personality as much as this because I've always been that kid like shut up with all the questions. I've always been that kid.
turns.zh: 反过来，我也能让这些人接触到正在收听和观看的其他所有人。这太不可思议了。对我来说，这就像完美的工作。我从未有过比这更符合我个性的工作，因为我一直是那个因为问题太多而被叫闭嘴的孩子。我一直是那个孩子。

turns.en: That's the system, right? It's not your fault. Like it's actually the reason you're successful now is the exact thing that people told you to shut up about in the past.
turns.zh: 这就是系统的问题，对吧？不是你的错。实际上，你现在成功的原因正是过去人们让你闭嘴的事情。

turns.en: Right?
turns.zh: 对吧？

turns.en: Like, 'Hey, stop bothering my lecture, asking all these unrelated questions.' It's mainly a frustration of the teacher that they don't have the answers to you.
turns.zh: 就像，‘嘿，别打扰我的课，问这些不相关的问题。’这主要是老师因为没有答案而感到沮丧。

turns.en: Sure.
turns.zh: 确实。

turns.en: And now that bottleneck is gone. We did this experiment with one instructor at MIT who taught the introduction to biology class. He came and told us that he's going to give Perplexity to all the students, and they would use it as part of the lectures. So instead of fighting AI, you just give AI to everybody and let them ask whatever questions they want, and they can actually use it in the exams too.
turns.zh: 现在这个瓶颈消失了。我们在麻省理工学院和一位教生物学入门课程的老师做了这个实验。他告诉我们，他会给所有学生提供 Perplexity，他们会在课堂上使用它。所以，与其对抗 AI，不如把 AI 交给每个人，让他们问任何问题，甚至在考试中也可以使用。

turns.en: So wow. So how do you even design questions for an exam in such a world? Maybe you just encourage people to pose a question that AI can't answer right now, and that becomes your research project, and you turn everybody into a scientist fundamentally.
turns.zh: 哇。那么在这样的世界里，你如何设计考试题目呢？也许你只是鼓励人们提出一个 AI 目前无法回答的问题，然后那成为他们的研究项目，你基本上把每个人都变成了科学家。

turns.en: There's this belief that scientists have to go through a rigorous PhD and get accredited by an amazing university to be that. But anyone who's curious can be a scientist. The only thing required to be a good scientist is intellectual humility: to understand that you could be wrong about things that everyone takes for granted, and when presented with new evidence and new data, you're willing to change your mind and operate with ambiguity and uncertainty about the world. That's basically all the qualities you need to be a scientist. You can run your experiments, gather data, gather evidence, consult people, bring in experts and talk to them. As long as you're uncovering more and more about the world, you are a scientist. You don't need a PhD to feel allowed to be a scientist. I think that's the most important quality we need to inculcate in our kids, the upcoming generation, so that they all feel more liberated. Finally, I don't have to memorize this textbook or these lecture materials, and I don't have to feel bad if I get 12 out of 20. Who cares? AI is always going to get 20 out of 20. That's not what you're meant to be good at. Of course, master the foundations, the basics. But your job is to actually pose interesting questions.
turns.zh: 有一种信念认为科学家必须经过严格的博士训练，并由一所了不起的大学认证。但任何好奇的人都可以成为科学家。成为一名好科学家唯一需要的是智识上的谦逊：理解你可能对大家认为理所当然的事情是错的，当面对新证据和新数据时，你愿意改变想法，并在世界的模糊和不确定性中运作。这基本上就是成为科学家所需的所有品质。你可以做实验、收集数据、收集证据、咨询他人、请专家来交谈。只要你在不断揭示世界的更多面貌，你就是科学家。你不需要博士学位才能觉得自己被允许成为科学家。我认为这是我们需要灌输给孩子们、下一代的最重要的品质，这样他们都会感到更解放。终于，我不必背诵这本教科书或这些讲义材料，如果我得了 12 分（满分 20 分）也不必感到难过。谁在乎呢？AI 总是能得 20 分。那不是你该擅长的事情。当然，要掌握基础、基本知识。但你的工作实际上是提出有趣的问题。

turns.en: Yeah. And the intellectual humility is so important because one of the things that was really weird about the whole COVID pandemic was that we weren't supposed to question science.
turns.zh: 是的。智识上的谦逊非常重要，因为整个新冠疫情期间一件非常奇怪的事情是我们不应该质疑科学。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: It was like when Fauci said if you question Anthony Fauci, you are questioning science.
turns.zh: 就像福奇说如果你质疑安东尼·福奇，你就是在质疑科学。

turns.en: That's because they try to assign credibility through their degrees, through their affiliations, but not through the scientific method. Anybody should be allowed to ask questions as long as they are open to new evidence.
turns.zh: 那是因为他们试图通过学位、通过隶属关系来赋予可信度，而不是通过科学方法。任何人都应该被允许提问，只要他们对新证据持开放态度。

turns.en: Right. And that's the most important quality of a scientist.
turns.zh: 对。那是科学家最重要的品质。

turns.en: Well, the scientific method alone is one of the most important things we can use to try to figure out what's real and what's not real. And as soon as someone says don't use it, don't question... Well, wait a minute. And then there was an actual government push to silence questioning, and legitimate researchers were kicked off Twitter because they didn't back the narrative. This is all anti-science. This is not questioning science. Science demands questioning.
turns.zh: 科学方法本身是我们用来分辨真实与不真实的最重要工具之一。一旦有人说不要用它，不要质疑……等等。然后政府实际上推动压制质疑，合法的研究人员因为不支持叙事而被踢出推特。这完全是反科学的。这不是在质疑科学。科学要求质疑。

turns.en: Yeah. When you don't understand something, the best thing you can do is ask all possible questions. And curbing that is almost like saying, 'Look, I'm going to tell you what happened. You need to believe in my worldview and I'm not open to new perspectives.'
turns.zh: 是的。当你不理解某件事时，最好的做法就是问所有可能的问题。而遏制这一点几乎就像在说，‘听着，我来告诉你发生了什么。你需要相信我的世界观，我对新观点不开放。’

turns.en: I wonder if anybody has used AI to try to map out possible scenarios for where technology leads human civilization and what could be done to mitigate the problems and push it in the proper direction, like have a bunch of different models of how this could play out.
turns.zh: 我想知道是否有人用 AI 来描绘技术引领人类文明的可能情景，以及可以做些什么来缓解问题并推动它走向正确的方向，比如建立一系列不同的模型来展示这可能如何发展。

turns.en: Yeah, I try to do that for fun, but I haven't done it in a serious enough way to have a proper answer to that. But I think a lot of things that we are doing today will not be considered needed or valuable. And maybe a little bit of taking our own lessons from the past. I don't know if when you grew up as a student, did you have to be good at mental math like multiplying arbitrary numbers? Was that considered a sign of smartness, or remembering people's phone numbers?
turns.zh: 是的，我为了好玩尝试过，但没有认真到能给出一个像样的答案。但我认为我们今天做的很多事情将来都不会被认为必要或有价值。也许稍微从过去吸取教训。我不知道你当学生的时候，是否必须擅长心算，比如任意数字的乘法？那被认为是聪明的标志吗？或者记住别人的电话号码？

turns.en: Well, you had to because there was a little address book that I kept on my desk. That's the only way I knew people's numbers.
turns.zh: 嗯，你必须这样做，因为有一个小地址本放在我桌上。那是我知道别人号码的唯一方式。

turns.en: And I remembered a bunch of them, like all my friends. I don't have any of my friends' numbers remembered now. Maybe my wife and my friend Eddie. I have two numbers in my head.
turns.zh: 我以前记得很多，比如所有朋友的号码。现在我一个朋友的号码都不记得了。也许我妻子和我的朋友埃迪的号码。我脑子里只有两个号码。

turns.en: But was there a time when people thought somebody was smart based on how good their memory power was?
turns.zh: 但有没有一个时期，人们根据记忆力好坏来判断一个人是否聪明？

turns.en: Oh yeah, definitely.
turns.zh: 哦，是的，当然。

turns.en: But would you say that now?
turns.zh: 但你现在会这么说吗？

turns.en: Well, people are impressed if you know things now. You know, I have a bunch of weird information obviously that I've gathered through so many years of doing this podcast and just so many years of being curious.
turns.zh: 嗯，现在如果你知道一些事情，人们会印象深刻。你知道，我显然有很多奇怪的信息，是通过这么多年做这个播客和这么多年保持好奇心收集来的。

turns.en: You know, sometimes even my own daughter is like, 'How do you know that?' I'm like, 'This is what I do.' That's my thing. I pay attention to stuff.
turns.zh: 你知道吗，有时候连我女儿都会问：‘你怎么知道的？’我说：‘这就是我的工作。’我就是干这个的。我关注细节。

turns.en: Memory itself is always very impressive. Someone has an excellent memory and can pull up facts of the past. We automatically equate that to intelligence.
turns.zh: 记忆力本身总是令人印象深刻。有人记忆力超群，能回忆起过去的事实。我们自动将其等同于智力。

turns.en: Yeah, I think it's impressive, but it's not necessarily a sign of being intelligent, right? It's just a very fast lookup table in your head. That's valuable, but being smart is about posing the most interesting questions.
turns.zh: 是的，我觉得这令人印象深刻，但不一定是聪明的标志，对吧？这只是你脑子里有一个非常快速的查找表。这很有价值，但聪明在于提出最有趣的问题。

turns.en: Also, the decisions you make and whether you self-correct when you make mistakes. All those things.
turns.zh: 还有你做出的决定，以及犯错时能否自我纠正。所有这些。

turns.en: Exactly. So, when you have an amplifier to your intelligence like AI, lookups are something you can delegate, reasoning for decision-making is something you can delegate. But posing the right questions, forming your own judgment based on what it reasons, and having the courage to make the decision—that's still you. That agency, that intrinsic curiosity, the scientific intellectual humility to gather new evidence and question your beliefs—that is still you. That is what will be considered smart in the ages to come.
turns.zh: 没错。所以，当你有了像 AI 这样的智力放大器，查找可以委托，决策推理也可以委托。但提出正确的问题，根据它的推理形成自己的判断，并有勇气做出决定——这仍然是你自己。那种能动性、内在的好奇心、收集新证据和质疑信念的科学谦逊——这仍然是你自己。这才是未来会被视为聪明的品质。

turns.en: If somebody is a proxy scientist, they don't have to go to MIT or Harvard and get a PhD to be considered a scientist, because all scientific literature is open and accessible. You can take a paper written by an expert, use AI to understand it deeply, ask questions, and maybe even disprove what they claim. That's the whole peer review process—questioning somebody's paper. Whatever you said happened in the old days is wrong. You should be allowed to ask questions about even eminent scientists' work. It's okay. If you're dumb and ask the wrong questions, you'll learn from that. It's worse than not being allowed to ask the question.
turns.zh: 如果某人是一个代理科学家，他们不必去麻省理工或哈佛拿博士学位才能被视为科学家，因为所有科学文献都是开放可及的。你可以拿一篇专家写的论文，用 AI 深入理解，提出问题，甚至可能反驳他们的主张。这就是整个同行评审过程——质疑某人的论文。你说过去发生的事是错的。你应该被允许质疑甚至著名科学家的工作。这没问题。如果你很笨，问了错误的问题，你会从中学习。这比不被允许提问更糟糕。

turns.en: Yeah, agreed. It's going to be interesting to see what the future of education looks like. How valuable are degrees when AI can do the majority of work?
turns.zh: 是的，同意。看看教育的未来会是什么样子会很有趣。当 AI 能完成大部分工作时，学位还有多大价值？

turns.en: How good are they now at law? You can ask questions, pretty amazing. How good are they at mathematics? Perfect. How good are they at coding? Way better than people.
turns.zh: 它们现在在法律方面有多好？你可以提问，非常惊人。数学方面呢？完美。编程方面呢？比人好得多。

turns.en: Yeah. At a certain point, it's going to be interesting: what is education now? Is it just providing information, because information is readily available? Or is it teaching you how to think? Teaching you how to pursue your interests, be curious, have intellectual humility, and understand what you know and what you don't know.
turns.zh: 是的。在某个时候，会很有趣：现在的教育是什么？它只是提供信息，因为信息随手可得？还是教你如何思考？教你如何追求兴趣、保持好奇、拥有知识谦逊，并理解你知道什么和不知道什么。

turns.en: I think that's what it should be. I still think institutions will preserve their brand value because there is a certain aspect of education outside of learning—just having access to other curious and intelligent people.
turns.zh: 我认为这应该是这样。我仍然认为机构会保持其品牌价值，因为教育有一个方面超出了学习本身——就是接触到其他好奇和聪明的人。

turns.en: Sure. Community.
turns.zh: 当然。社区。

turns.en: Yeah. Brands attract good communities, peer groups, etc. But the actual process of learning has to change, and what you're rewarded for has to change. Everything flows downstream of the incentive. If the incentive is to score highest on an exam based on answers, you're not changing much. To change that process, you need to change what you reward a student—what is an A+ or A. That's where we need to start.
turns.zh: 是的。品牌吸引好的社区、同伴群体等等。但实际的学习过程必须改变，你被奖励的东西也必须改变。一切都源于激励。如果激励是基于答案在考试中得最高分，那你没改变多少。要改变这个过程，你需要改变奖励学生的标准——什么是 A+或 A。这才是我们需要开始的地方。

turns.en: Well, we talked about this the other day: the education system in this country was designed to make workers, and the curriculum was designed around that.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们前几天讨论过：这个国家的教育体系是为了培养工人而设计的，课程也是围绕这个设计的。

turns.en: In India, it's still the case, by the way. Even if you go into a computer science degree, at least when I was there, the first two years you spend learning hardcore electrical and mechanical engineering—welding, using lathe machines, workshops, carpentry. It was fun in hindsight, learning soldering and making circuits. But if someone was just interested in writing code, all this was pointless. You had to go through it to be qualified as an engineer. The curriculum was designed that way because that's what the labor force required back then—to build oil factories, etc. So you had to learn mechanical engineering, fluid mechanics. That should also change. If the way you do work changes, what you're trained for in college should change. It's much harder to change these things. People are slower, scared of change. Disruption is looked down upon. So let's start at the incentive structure from schools and colleges—not reward people based on how much they know.
turns.zh: 顺便说一句，在印度仍然如此。即使你攻读计算机科学学位，至少在我那时候，前两年你要学习硬核的电气和机械工程——焊接、使用车床、车间、木工。事后看来很有趣，学习焊接和制作电路。但如果有人只对写代码感兴趣，所有这些都毫无意义。你必须经历这些才能成为合格的工程师。课程这样设计是因为当时劳动力需要这些——建造炼油厂等等。所以你必须学习机械工程、流体力学。这也应该改变。如果工作方式变了，大学培训的内容也应该变。改变这些事情要难得多。人们行动缓慢，害怕改变。颠覆总是被看不起。所以让我们从学校和学院的激励结构开始——不要根据人们知道多少来奖励他们。

turns.en: Well, it seems like in the future, when things radically change—and they seem inevitable—universities and schools will be rewarded for developing thinkers who can adapt to this new world. They'll have to figure out how to adjust their curriculum. Because the tools are so spectacular now that just memorizing information is not what you'll need to get by.
turns.zh: 嗯，看起来在未来，当事情发生根本性变化时——而且这似乎不可避免——大学和学校会因为培养出能够适应这个新世界的思考者而获得回报。他们将不得不弄清楚如何调整课程。因为现在的工具如此惊人，仅仅记忆信息并不是你未来生存所需要的。

turns.en: It's not.
turns.zh: 确实不是。

turns.en: And I guess one proxy different schools use is maybe if more entrepreneurs arise out of your school, you probably created a lot of independent thinkers.
turns.zh: 我想不同学校使用的一个指标是，如果你的学校走出了更多创业者，那你可能培养了很多独立思考者。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Because they are willing to take a fresh perspective towards a problem, right? And build their own thing from scratch.
turns.zh: 因为他们愿意用新视角看问题，对吧？然后从零开始打造自己的东西。

turns.en: And fundamentally, that's what America has always been about: the American dream of coming here, having your own idea, and still being taken seriously by a bunch of people. The whole idea of venture capital, or family and friends helping you bootstrap a business and turning it into a success. Success doesn't mean multi-billion or 10 billion, right? As long as it pays you enough that you don't have to work for somebody else, you can live a fulfilling life, and go explore your passions—that's success. That's actually a better success than creating a company based on what other people want you to do and then hating your job for it.
turns.zh: 从根本上说，美国一直是这样：美国梦就是来到这里，有自己的想法，并且被一群人认真对待。风险投资的概念，或者家人朋友帮你白手起家，把生意做成功。成功并不意味着几十亿或百亿，对吧？只要它能让你不必为别人工作，过上充实的生活，去探索你的热情所在——那就是成功。这实际上比按照别人期望你做的事去创办公司、然后讨厌自己的工作要好得多。

turns.en: Yeah. And having a yacht and being miserable and working every day.
turns.zh: 是啊，有游艇却每天痛苦地工作。

turns.en: And that's why I said the smartest or richest people are not always the ones with the most fulfilling lives. The most curious people have the most fulfilling lives because they have better relationships. They're actually able to sit and look at something and be curious about it instead of worrying about what's going on.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么我说最聪明或最富有的人并不总是拥有最充实的生活。最有好奇心的人拥有最充实的生活，因为他们有更好的人际关系。他们能够坐下来观察某件事，对它感到好奇，而不是担心发生了什么。

transcript.sec: The American Dream and Risk-Seeking Culture

turns.en: What did the American dream mean to you when you weren't in America? How is it discussed?
turns.zh: 当你不在美国时，美国梦对你意味着什么？它是怎么被讨论的？

turns.en: Well, to me, I always thought America is the only country where you can come here, have an idea, and people listen to you and encourage you to go pursue it. The risk-seeking culture is just incredible here. Everywhere else, you are either explicitly or implicitly forced to defer to authority. You have to ask permission from this person or that person, get their approval or insight. But if you have a thought that challenges what they believe, this country still encourages you to go pursue it. So yes, when I came here, obviously Google was the number one company everybody wanted to work for, but it's also the same country that allows a new person to start a new idea that challenges one of the biggest companies in this country, and actually wants it. People actually want new ideas. You can consistently see that there will always be more new ideas and new companies created here. That spirit of questioning is encouraged a lot here. It happens in academic research too. I started off as an academic, and even there, when I had ideas and shared them, people gave very honest feedback but didn't stop me from working on anything. That's fantastic because it's very fresh and liberating.
turns.zh: 对我来说，我一直认为美国是唯一一个你可以来到这里，有了一个想法，人们会倾听并鼓励你去追求它的国家。这里的冒险文化简直不可思议。在其他任何地方，你都会或明或暗地被要求服从权威。你必须去征求这个人的许可，那个人的认可或见解。但如果你有一个挑战他们信仰的想法，这个国家仍然鼓励你去追求它。所以是的，当我来到这里时，显然谷歌是每个人都想去的头号公司，但这也是同一个国家，允许一个新来者提出一个挑战本国最大公司之一的新想法，并且实际上渴望它。人们确实想要新想法。你可以持续看到，这里总是会有更多的新想法和新公司被创造出来。这种质疑的精神在这里受到很大鼓励。在学术研究中也是如此。我最初是学术界出身，即使在那里，当我有想法并分享时，人们会给出非常诚实的反馈，但不会阻止我做任何事。这太棒了，因为它非常新鲜，令人解放。

turns.en: And that's not anywhere else?
turns.zh: 而其他地方没有？

turns.en: I would say it's not. It's not in India.
turns.zh: 我会说没有。印度没有。

turns.en: It's a simplification to say it's not anywhere else, but it's not as encouraged.
turns.zh: 说其他地方都没有是简化了，但确实不那么受鼓励。

turns.en: It's not as encouraged. The incentive structures are not quite there, and the ability to be taken seriously for some crazy ideas is why America is still at the top.
turns.zh: 确实不那么受鼓励。激励结构不太到位，而因为一些疯狂的想法被认真对待的能力，正是美国仍然处于顶峰的原因。

transcript.sec: Why Other Countries Don't Adopt American Values

turns.en: But it's crazy to me that if the American dream is so compelling and so many people come here for it, why doesn't the rest of the world sort of adopt those values?
turns.zh: 但让我觉得疯狂的是，如果美国梦如此有吸引力，那么多人为此而来，为什么世界其他地方不采纳这些价值观呢？

turns.en: It's hard. A lot of it is cultural. America was born from a piece of land essentially. A lot of ideas and industries we built here were created from nothing, and that required taking bold risks. I think Jeff Bezos said in some podcast: where else would you be able to raise a few million dollars for an idea with a 5 to 10% chance of working, fail at it, and still go raise another few million for your next idea? Nowhere else. People who get rich here actually want to encourage and be part of somebody else's crazy journey because it's hard to pursue all crazy bets yourself. So it's an ecosystem, and once something becomes an ecosystem, there are network effects. So it's very hard to copy that elsewhere.
turns.zh: 这很难。很大程度上是文化问题。美国基本上是从一片土地诞生的。我们在这里建立的许多想法和产业都是从无到有创造出来的，这需要承担巨大的风险。我记得杰夫·贝佐斯在某个播客里说过：还有哪里能让你为一个成功概率只有 5%到 10%的想法筹集几百万美元，失败之后还能再为下一个想法筹集几百万？没有其他地方。在这里致富的人实际上想要鼓励并参与别人的疯狂旅程，因为你自己很难去追求所有疯狂的赌注。所以这是一个生态系统，一旦成为生态系统，就有网络效应。所以很难在其他地方复制。

transcript.secZh: 他国为何不采纳美国价值观

transcript.sec: Value in Curiosity and Hard Work

turns.en: And so your value is measured in your curiosity and your willingness to work on whatever it is. Yes. That is your pursuit.
turns.zh: 所以你的价值取决于你的好奇心和你愿意为之努力的事情。是的，那就是你的追求。

turns.en: Yes. And then eventually adjusting and learning and catching fire with one of them.
turns.zh: 是的。然后最终调整、学习，并在其中一件事上点燃火花。

turns.en: Correct. And you have to work hard. I am a big believer in intense hard work. I think nothing great can be accomplished by being soft. So all this recent push for work-life balance—sure, if that's what you want, and there are certain jobs that give you that. But when you're trying to do something from scratch, when you're trying to create something from nothing, it's not meant to be easy. There are some sacrifices that have to be made. You're signing up to be part of that experience, that surreal joy from doing something that felt almost impossible to achieve. You're not staying up late or waking up early because you're getting paid more. Maybe you might not get paid anything. Maybe this whole thing goes to nothing. But that experience of being part of something that feels very hard to achieve is what you're signing up for.
turns.zh: 没错。你必须努力工作。我坚信高强度的努力。我认为没有什么伟大的成就是靠软弱取得的。所以最近所有关于工作与生活平衡的推动——当然，如果你想要，有些工作可以给你。但当你试图从零开始，从无到有创造时，它本就不该是容易的。必须做出一些牺牲。你是在签约成为那种体验的一部分，那种从几乎不可能实现的事情中获得的超现实喜悦。你熬夜或早起不是因为报酬更高。也许你一分钱也拿不到。也许整件事都会化为乌有。但那种参与一件很难实现的事情的体验，才是你签约的目的。

turns.en: Yeah. And if you're not, find something else. It's fine. Respect that. Nothing wrong with that.
turns.zh: 是啊。如果你不是这样，那就找点别的。没关系。尊重这一点。这没什么不对。

turns.en: Exactly. And the country has enough jobs to provide for all kinds of needs, right? Everybody goes through different phases in their life. Sometimes they feel a little lazy or disillusioned. So what I like about this country is that there are a lot of curious people here. There are so many different people, whether they use AI or not, all finding meaning in so many interesting projects.
turns.zh: 完全正确。这个国家有足够的工作来满足各种需求，对吧？每个人都会经历人生的不同阶段。有时他们会感到有点懒惰或幻想破灭。所以我喜欢这个国家的是，这里有很多好奇的人。有这么多不同的人，无论他们是否使用 AI，都在这么多有趣的项目中寻找意义。

transcript.sec: The American Dream and Underdog Culture

turns.en: Well, obviously I don't know any other country really because I was born here, but the people that do talk to me about what the American dream is like from another country, they're the most passionate and the most supportive of this idea, this experiment in self-government and the whole idea that the country operates on that anybody can chase their dream, that if you have a dream and you're willing to work hard, you could actually do it in this country.
turns.zh: 嗯，显然我不了解其他国家，因为我出生在这里，但那些从国外跟我谈论美国梦的人，他们是最热情、最支持这个理念的——这个自治实验，以及整个国家运作的基础：任何人都可以追逐梦想，如果你有梦想并愿意努力，你确实能在这个国家实现它。
没错。
turns.en: That's right.

Yeah. The people most passionate about that idea often come from somewhere else where that wasn't available. And it's not just people from one particular country; it's the attitude, the way the system works and rewards you for being bold and taking bets against established players. It's okay to be an upstart, a challenger. People love the underdog. That culture is continuing. Yes, there are multi-trillion dollar companies here, but people still want the young hungry person to also be successful.
turns.zh: 是的。对这个理念最热情的人往往来自那些无法实现它的地方。而且这不只是来自某个特定国家的人；这是一种态度，是系统运作的方式，它奖励你大胆挑战老牌玩家。你可以是后起之秀、挑战者。人们喜欢弱者。这种文化仍在延续。是的，这里有市值数万亿美元的公司，但人们仍然希望年轻有干劲的人也能成功。
是的。他们喜欢颠覆者。
turns.en: Yeah. Well, they love disruptors.

And people love underdogs in this country. It's universal, not specific to technology. I'm sure everybody would love an underdog story that wants to go against Coca-Cola or Pepsi too. In sports, it's our favorite thing. We don't like when the guy who's supposed to win wins; we love when the guy who's not supposed to win triumphs. The underdog story is very uniquely American to me. That's what this country is. Sure, there are obstacles and challenges like every other country, but it's one thing that has consistently stayed true.
turns.zh: 这个国家的人喜欢弱者。这是普遍的，不限于科技。我相信每个人都会喜欢挑战可口可乐或百事可乐的弱者故事。在体育界，这是我们最喜欢的事。我们不喜欢本该赢的人赢；我们喜欢不该赢的人获胜。弱者故事对我来说非常独特地属于美国。这就是这个国家的本质。当然，和其他国家一样，这里有障碍和挑战，但这一点始终如一。

transcript.sec: Concerns About Big Tech and Data Control

turns.en: One of the big fears that people in America have about technology in particular is that without being aware, everybody gave up their data and didn't recognize it was a commodity. That in turn made these corporations immensely wealthy and powerful. And then they have the ability to shape narratives. That concerns people because they use their ideological position as leverage to push through technology that has immense control and influence over people. We didn't see technology and corporations as having that much control over how society views itself and how we interact with each other. There's a real concern that these companies got so big. There's a guy named Robert Epstein who's done a lot of work on curated search engine results and how much that can affect elections and people's perceptions on any societal issue. It's concerning because they do curate search results. It's not simply raw data; if you look for specific political figures, depending on where they fall on the spectrum and the company's leanings, you'll get different results. That sucks. It's very concerning that people don't recognize how dangerous that is for society, to have that kind of power and wield it in a way that's not honest about accurate objective information, but pushes particular ideologies.
turns.zh: 美国人特别担心科技的一个大问题是，在不知情的情况下，每个人都交出了自己的数据，没有意识到它是一种商品。这反过来让这些公司变得极其富有和强大。然后他们有能力塑造叙事。这让人担忧，因为他们利用自己的意识形态立场作为杠杆，通过技术对人们施加巨大的控制和影响。我们以前没有意识到科技和公司对社会如何看待自己以及我们如何互动有这么大的控制力。人们真正担心这些公司变得如此庞大。有一个叫罗伯特·爱泼斯坦的人做了很多关于策划搜索引擎结果的研究，以及这能在多大程度上影响选举和人们对任何社会问题的看法。这令人担忧，因为他们确实策划搜索结果。不是简单的原始数据；如果你搜索特定的政治人物，根据他们所处的光谱和公司的倾向，你会得到不同的结果。这很糟糕。人们没有意识到这种权力对社会有多危险，以不诚实的方式使用它，不提供准确客观的信息，而是推动特定的意识形态。

transcript.secZh: 对大科技与数据控制的担忧

turns.en: Yeah. So I think it's kind of an effect of the asymmetry between the amount of AI power that centralized systems and companies have and the amount of AI power you as a sovereign individual have. When you don't have the AIs to judge for yourself what you should be reading, you're under the influence of whatever big tech company controls the information. But when you have access to all those AIs, you can customize what you want to see by telling the AI, 'Hey, this is what I think, you should actually question and tell me.' Until now, you never had that power for yourself. You're finally getting it. Eventually we'll be able to have our own LLMs, models we can host on our own hardware. We don't have to rely on one centralized model from a specific model company. You can shape it to your beliefs, your custom data. When consuming a search result, you can ask that AI you control, which nobody can shut off, to give you a contrarian perspective or tell you if the search results are biased. So we need to give individuals more sovereignty with more access to their own AIs that they own and run on hardware they own. This will lead to the rise of local AIs. Today, AI models are very power-efficient and run on large data centers. But in a year or two, whatever capability exists in the most power-hungry data centers will be possible to run in a box you own.
turns.zh: 是的。所以我认为这有点像中心化系统和公司拥有的 AI 能力与你作为独立个体拥有的 AI 能力之间的不对称效应。当你没有 AI 来自行判断应该读什么时，你就受制于控制信息的大科技公司。但当你能够使用所有这些 AI 时，你可以通过告诉 AI‘嘿，这是我认为的，你应该质疑并告诉我’来定制你想看的内容。直到现在，你从未拥有这种力量。你终于得到了它。最终我们将能够拥有自己的 LLM，即我们可以托管在自己硬件上的模型。我们不必依赖某个特定模型公司的中心化模型。你可以根据你的信仰、你的自定义数据来塑造它。在消费搜索结果时，你可以询问你控制的 AI（没有人能关闭它）给你一个相反的观点，或者告诉你搜索结果是否有偏见。所以我们需要赋予个人更多主权，让他们能够访问自己拥有并在自己硬件上运行的 AI。这将导致本地 AI 的兴起。今天，AI 模型非常节能，运行在大型数据中心。但在一两年内，目前最耗电的数据中心所具备的能力将可以在你拥有的一个盒子里运行。

turns.en: It's already happening. There are interesting hardware projects like the Apple Mac Mini, Nvidia DGX, where you can host a reasonable size model and put it in a box, run it without paying for all the tokens. You just plug it into your power cord and it works. I know Duncan Trussell does that.
turns.zh: 这已经在发生了。有一些有趣的硬件项目，比如 Apple Mac Mini、Nvidia DGX，你可以托管一个合理大小的模型，把它放在一个盒子里运行，无需为所有 token 付费。你只需插上电源线就能工作。我知道邓肯·特鲁塞尔就这么做。

turns.en: Yeah. Today the capability of that locally running model is not quite there, so you'd still prefer something from a data center. But eventually this will be a spectrum. Some percentage of tasks will be delegated to the local system. It'll be a hybrid model. Over time, you could buy something that feels like a refrigerator for your home, which is your own AI box, and host a model you control.
turns.zh: 是的。今天本地运行模型的能力还不太够，所以你仍然会倾向于使用数据中心的东西。但最终这将是一个光谱。一定比例的任务会委托给本地系统。这将是一个混合模型。随着时间的推移，你可以买一个像冰箱一样的东西放在家里，那就是你自己的 AI 盒子，托管一个你控制的模型。

transcript.secZh: 用本地 AI 赋能个人

turns.en: So nobody can arbitrarily shut off access to it one day. And then you can have that be your weapon against what the big tech wants you to be fed or believe in. This is the only way we can fight this because they have far more computing power, far more data, far more algorithms than you. So the only way you can fight that is you have something you own yourself. And with the rise of open-source models, open-source LLMs, you can just progress on local hardware. And both Apple, Nvidia, Intel, they're all doing amazing work here. You could potentially change the future and give people more power. And this may not be as expensive as those people think.
turns.zh: 所以没有人能某天随意切断它的访问。然后你可以把它当作对抗大科技公司想让你接受或相信的东西的武器。这是唯一能对抗的方式，因为他们拥有比你多得多的算力、数据和算法。所以你唯一能对抗的方法就是拥有自己掌控的东西。随着开源模型和开源大语言模型的兴起，你可以在本地硬件上取得进展。苹果、英伟达、英特尔都在这里做着出色的工作。你有可能改变未来，赋予人们更多权力。而且这可能并不像那些人想象的那么昂贵。

turns.en: Well that's a good solution because I've always wondered, is using Google going to be irrelevant one day? Because you already can just ask your phone. I most of the time, if I want to have an answer for something, I just ask Perplexity. Instead of having to sift through all these Google searches and try to figure out what it's showing me first and get to page three where it's what I really want to know, I can get the accurate information, then follow-up questions are instantaneous.
turns.zh: 这是个好办法，因为我一直好奇，用谷歌搜索有一天会过时吗？因为你现在已经可以直接问手机了。大多数时候，如果我想知道什么答案，我就直接问 Perplexity。不用再翻遍所有谷歌搜索结果，试图弄明白它先给我看什么，然后翻到第三页才找到我真正想知道的。我能直接得到准确信息，然后追问也是即时的。

turns.en: Yeah. And even the models that are running the Perplexity app today, they're all in the cloud. Eventually, you'll be able to do that on a box that you own. You can still use the front end, the UI of the app, but you can control the compute that runs on a piece of hardware. You may ask why do I care? Okay. Like what if someday the data center gets taken off, like Iran was bombing data centers? Or like what if someday the government decides that model is no longer available? You want some control over what models you can run, and you may even want to shape it to your context that you never want to be living on any data center. And I think that's where I believe the individual gets more sovereignty against big tech. And that's how we fight the surveillance or centralization of power.
turns.zh: 是的。即使是今天运行 Perplexity 应用的模型，也都在云端。最终，你将能在自己拥有的设备上做到这一点。你仍然可以使用应用的前端界面，但你可以控制运行在硬件上的算力。你可能会问，我为什么在乎？好吧。比如有一天数据中心被摧毁了，就像伊朗轰炸数据中心那样？或者有一天政府决定某个模型不再可用？你希望对自己能运行哪些模型有一定控制权，甚至可能希望根据你的情境来塑造它，你永远不想让它存在于任何数据中心。我认为这就是个人获得更多主权以对抗大科技公司的地方。这就是我们对抗监控或权力集中的方式。

turns.en: Yeah. And certainly pushing narratives. What do you think happens with social media? Because social media, as you were talking about before, algorithms, it's one of the biggest problems in terms of the way people view the world. I'm curious what you think. My opinion is that it's not good for the kids.
turns.zh: 是的，而且肯定在推动叙事。你对社交媒体怎么看？因为社交媒体，就像你之前谈到的，算法，是人们看待世界方式的最大问题之一。我很好奇你的想法。我的观点是它对孩子不好。

turns.en: It's terrible for them.
turns.zh: 对他们来说很糟糕。

turns.en: Yeah. But I think they should have some exposure to it because I think it's good to know that it's a thing. And I think children are fairly resilient and they learn. But the anxiety levels of kids is much higher than ever before. Suicidal ideation's higher, self harm.
turns.zh: 是的。但我认为他们应该接触一些，因为知道有这个东西是好的。而且我认为孩子相当有韧性，他们会学习。但孩子们的焦虑水平比以往任何时候都高。自杀意念更高，自残也更多。

turns.en: Yeah. My belief is that when you're just fed a feed and the algorithm of the social media company decides what you're going to see next, it curbs your curiosity. And I don't think things that curb human curiosity should be encouraged.
turns.zh: 是的。我的看法是，当你只是被动接收信息流，而社交媒体公司的算法决定你接下来看到什么时，它会抑制你的好奇心。我认为抑制人类好奇心的东西不应该被鼓励。

turns.en: Yeah, I agree. And so if the app is designed in a way where it asks you what you're interested in and helps you to come up and find things that are very related to what you're interested in, that's awesome. But that's not how it works. It's literally like it starts with something, you start doom scrolling, and then it starts showing you what you just scrolled, and then you end up in an echo chamber. And that's not necessarily good.
turns.zh: 是的，我同意。所以如果应用的设计方式是询问你感兴趣的内容，并帮助你发现与你兴趣非常相关的东西，那很棒。但现实并非如此。它基本上是从某样东西开始，你开始无休止地刷，然后它开始展示你刚刚刷过的内容，最终你陷入一个回音室。这不一定好。

turns.en: Well, you can get trapped. Yeah, you can get in a trap of schizophrenics lately on Instagram, which is mostly schizophrenics, like people that tell they're the rightful president of the United States and you tell the guy hasn't showered in days. And if you have a phone, you can create an account and you just start uploading nonsense. And then for whatever reason, I've watched a couple of them. So now they just keep showing them to me. And it's full of AI slop right now. A lot of AI, it's not even clear. And it's not labeled clearly whether it's been made with AI or not. So essentially it's leading to a complete loss in trust where when I see something I don't even know if it's real anymore.
turns.zh: 嗯，你可能会被困住。是的，最近在 Instagram 上你可能会陷入精神分裂症患者的陷阱，大多是精神分裂症患者，比如那些自称是美国合法总统的人，而且你能看出那人好几天没洗澡了。如果你有手机，你可以创建一个账号，然后开始上传废话。然后不知为何，我看了几个，现在它们就一直推给我。而且现在上面全是 AI 垃圾。很多 AI 内容，甚至不清楚是不是 AI 做的。而且也没有明确标注是否由 AI 生成。所以这基本上导致信任完全丧失，当我看到某样东西时，我甚至不知道它是否真实。

turns.en: Right, and it's going to get worse.
turns.zh: 没错，而且情况会变得更糟。

turns.en: Yeah, it's going to get worse to the extent that you're going to like your default would be that this is AI, and then you're going to have to go through multiple layers to finally verify if it was. And even verified accounts post a lot of AI stuff. So it's not about whether the account is verified by Meta or some or whatever, right? So I think fundamentally, the way I think about it is: what are pieces of technology that if they did not exist would be a really bad thing for the world, and what are pieces of technology that if they did not exist wouldn't even matter? And I feel like social media is more towards the second. Like, searching for information and answering questions and getting AIs to do things for you, help you learn new things faster, all that stuff is something we need more of. Because it supercharges our curiosity. Whereas brain rot feeds with AI slop doesn't actually supercharge our curiosity. It actually curbs our curiosity. And so if we believe in the curiosity premium idea, we need to encourage things that supercharge our curiosity and discourage things that curb our curiosity.
turns.zh: 是的，情况会变得更糟，以至于你的默认反应会是这是 AI 生成的，然后你需要经过多层验证才能最终确认。甚至经过验证的账号也会发布很多 AI 内容。所以问题不在于账号是否被 Meta 或其他平台验证，对吧？所以我认为从根本上说，我的思考方式是：哪些技术如果不存在对世界来说会是件坏事，哪些技术如果不存在根本无关紧要？我觉得社交媒体更接近后者。比如，搜索信息、回答问题、让 AI 为你做事、帮助你更快学习新东西，所有这些我们都需要更多。因为它们能激发我们的好奇心。而用 AI 垃圾填满大脑的信息流实际上并不能激发好奇心，反而会抑制好奇心。所以如果我们相信好奇心溢价这个理念，我们就需要鼓励那些激发好奇心的事物，抑制那些抑制好奇心的事物。

turns.en: Do you anticipate a time where we recognize the dangers of algorithms and there is some discussion to either curb them or allow people to have control over them in a real meaningful way? Like you could dictate maybe through AI even that there's an AI interface to your algorithm that understands your particular emotional needs, your curiosity. Only show me this is what I'm interested in: carpentry and basketball games. Show me those. I don't want to see who's getting divorced. I don't give a about this.
turns.zh: 你预见到一个时刻，我们会认识到算法的危险，并进行一些讨论，要么限制它们，要么让人们以真正有意义的方式控制它们？比如，你甚至可以通过 AI 来指示，有一个 AI 界面来管理你的算法，它理解你特定的情感需求和好奇心。只给我看这些我感兴趣的东西：木工和篮球比赛。给我看这些。我不想看谁在离婚。我根本不在乎这些。

turns.en: Yeah. So, here's the thing. You can still customize on most of these social apps. You know, if you it'll be deeply buried somewhere in the settings somewhere and you can go and say stuff.
turns.zh: 是的。所以，事情是这样的。在大多数社交应用上你仍然可以自定义。你知道，它会被深埋在设置的某个地方，你可以去设置一些东西。

turns.en: But the reason it's buried is because once you always have to say it, or like it's the starting entry point for your experience there, your engagement time would go down because once you consume the content that you really want, you would go back to your work, which is what you really need to be doing.
turns.zh: 但之所以被埋没，是因为你总是得说出来，或者它就是你体验的起点，你的参与时间会下降，因为一旦你消费了真正想要的内容，你就会回去工作，那才是你真正需要做的。

turns.en: Right?
turns.zh: 对吧？

turns.en: But that doesn't help them sell more ads, right? And so the incentives are not aligned. And so Elon has this really good metric he talks about where it's like total amount of unregretted minutes spent on the app should go up.
turns.zh: 但这并不能帮他们卖更多广告，对吧？所以激励是不一致的。埃隆有一个很好的指标，他提到说，用户在应用上花费的“无悔分钟”总量应该增加。

turns.en: That's a good question. It's hard to measure. It's hard to measure.
turns.zh: 这是个好问题。很难衡量。很难衡量。

turns.en: It's more like in spirit the right metric.
turns.zh: 这更像是一种精神上正确的指标。

turns.en: But this metric is also why it's hard to make money on ads if you care about this metric. Which is why X doesn't really make a lot of money on ads compared to, you know, Instagram or YouTube, right?
turns.zh: 但这个指标也正是为什么如果你在乎它，就很难通过广告赚钱。这就是为什么 X 在广告收入上比不上 Instagram 或 YouTube，对吧？

turns.en: Because you're kind of optimizing for interestingness, but that doesn't mean X has everything, right? There's a lot of chaos, a lot of memes, a lot of weird going on there as well. But in general, social media is not necessarily great for people. I think it's terrible for people, but it also provides you with a way better understanding of what's going on in the world than has ever existed before.
turns.zh: 因为你是在优化趣味性，但这并不意味着 X 什么都有，对吧？那里也有很多混乱、表情包和奇怪的东西。但总的来说，社交媒体对人并不一定好。我认为它对人很糟糕，但它也让你比以往任何时候都更好地了解世界上正在发生的事情。

turns.en: X particularly
turns.zh: 尤其是 X

turns.en: Because it's a place for discourse. It's a text-based app more than a video-based app, right? So naturally people tend to engage in discussions and debates, and there's a lot of curious debates going on there and a lot of interesting viewpoints expressed by people. So I think in terms of the unregretted minutes, it's actually one of the better social media apps. But apps that are purely based on video or images, and largely video these days, I think that's just trying to get your eyeballs in time.
turns.zh: 因为这是一个讨论的地方。它更多是一个基于文本的应用，而不是视频，对吧？所以自然人们倾向于参与讨论和辩论，那里有很多有趣的辩论和人们表达的有趣观点。所以我认为在“无悔分钟”方面，它实际上是更好的社交媒体应用之一。但那些纯粹基于视频或图像的应用，尤其是现在大部分是视频，我认为那只是试图及时抓住你的眼球。

turns.en: Yeah. Those are the mind numbers. Yeah, they just numb your mind.
turns.zh: 是啊。那些是麻木心智的数字。是啊，它们只是让你的大脑麻木。

turns.en: I mean, it's depressing when you go into a metro and you just see people just scrolling through their feed. Nobody—

turns.en: Everybody doing it. You look the entire car, everyone's doing it. It's just insane.

turns.en: Yeah. It's weird. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。很奇怪。是啊。

turns.en: I always say that if there was a drug that existed that made people stare at their hand for six hours a day, everybody would be like, 'Get that out of here.' But that's essentially what we're doing because most of what people are looking at most of the time, they don't even remember.
turns.zh: 我常说，如果有一种药让人每天盯着自己的手看六个小时，每个人都会说‘把它拿走’。但这基本上就是我们在做的，因为人们大部分时间看的东西，他们甚至都不记得。

turns.en: Yeah. They're just scrolling through this thing. It's brain rot. It curbs your curiosity.
turns.zh: 是啊。他们只是刷着这东西。这是脑腐。它抑制了你的好奇心。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: I mean, Apple has these settings in different apps. Have you tried this where you can set the timer for every app?
turns.zh: 我的意思是，苹果在不同应用里有这些设置。你试过给每个应用设置计时器吗？

turns.en: No, I just use discipline. I don't engage very much anymore. I dip my toe into X every day for a few seconds. I go, what's everybody mad at? What's going on? Who stole this? How much corruption's here? Who got killed there? Okay, bye. And then I just check out. I don't want to do it. And Instagram to me is just nonsense. I just look at that every now and then for nonsense and occasionally something interesting.
turns.zh: 没有，我就靠自律。我不怎么参与了。我每天在 X 上浅尝几秒钟。我看看，大家在生什么气？发生什么了？谁偷了这个？这里有多少腐败？那里谁被杀了？好了，再见。然后我就退出了。我不想做这个。Instagram 对我来说就是胡扯。我只是偶尔看看那些胡扯，偶尔有点有趣的东西。

turns.en: Really? YouTube is my main go-to thing. Yeah. Because YouTube is my most unregretted minutes.
turns.zh: 真的吗？YouTube 是我的主要去处。是啊。因为 YouTube 是我“无悔分钟”最多的。

turns.en: Yeah. YouTube for me is always interesting. There's always some cool thing on cosmology. There's some— I watch fights on YouTube. I watch professional pool matches. That's what I do for the most part. I that's where I really find my actual interests and fulfill my curiosity.
turns.zh: 是啊。YouTube 对我来说总是很有趣。总有一些关于宇宙学的酷东西。还有一些——我在 YouTube 上看打架。我看职业台球比赛。那是我大部分时间做的事。那是我真正找到自己兴趣并满足好奇心的所在。

turns.en: Long form content is what human mind should be trained to consume more of. Whether it's books, whether it's like, you know, 30 minute videos explaining something.
turns.zh: 长内容是人类大脑应该训练去更多消费的。无论是书籍，还是像三十分钟的解释视频。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: And you need to train your mind to actually complete it. That's actually the biggest problem with the younger generation. The more they're in the reals experience short form video, they're unable to actually complete long videos anymore.
turns.zh: 而且你需要训练你的大脑去真正完成它。这实际上是年轻一代最大的问题。他们越是沉浸在短视频体验中，就越无法完成长视频了。

turns.en: That's true. But also at the same time the rise of podcast is happening.
turns.zh: 确实如此。但与此同时，播客也在兴起。

turns.en: Yeah. And it's great. It's great.
turns.zh: 是啊。这很好。很好。

turns.en: So there's— it's not universal. It's like there's a lot of people that don't find fulfillment in all the doom scrolling and all the nonsense and they really do want—
turns.zh: 所以——这并非普遍现象。有很多人并不在无休止的刷屏和胡扯中找到满足，他们真的想要——

turns.en: Yeah. I'm particularly focused on the younger generation. I'm sure people like us can adapt, like okay let's say maybe I have a temporary addiction to social apps and we can—
turns.zh: 是啊。我特别关注年轻一代。我相信像我们这样的人可以适应，比如好吧，也许我暂时对社交应用上瘾，我们可以——

turns.en: But a lot of the young people are— the people like I meet kids at the mall that are 11 that listen to my podcast.
turns.zh: 但很多年轻人是——比如我在商场遇到 11 岁的孩子听我的播客。

turns.en: Really?
turns.zh: 真的吗？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: Wow.
turns.zh: 哇。

turns.en: I know it's nuts. They go 'I love your podcast.' I'm like 'Who lets you listen? Get out of here.' No, I'm always joking around about it. Like it's really cool.
turns.zh: 我知道这很疯狂。他们说‘我喜欢你的播客’。我说‘谁让你听的？走开。’不，我总在开玩笑。其实真的很酷。

turns.en: But no, there's a lot of particularly young boys that come up to me all the time that are interested in it. That's amazing.
turns.zh: 但确实有很多年轻男孩经常来找我，他们对这个感兴趣。太棒了。

turns.en: I love it. I love it because then they're going to get exposed to some interesting ideas and it'll also encourage them to have those kind of conversations with each other, right?
turns.zh: 我喜欢。我喜欢是因为这样他们就能接触到一些有趣的想法，也会鼓励他们彼此进行这样的对话，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.zh: 你听谁的播客？

turns.en: I love Tim Dillon's. He's probably my favorite because it's the most accurate and also satirical and hilarious view on everything that's going on in the world in terms of war and world news and culture. And he's my favorite. He was just on here yesterday. I love that guy to death. He's so funny. He's so crazy. It's like his mind works in such a unique way and it's developed because his podcast is different where he very rarely has guests. So most of the time it's just him ranting and his producer laughing, and he's the best ranter that's ever lived. I don't think there's anybody that's even close. He's the GOAT. I listen to a lot of archery shows and hunting shows where they're talking about different tactics in hunting or different techniques in archery, new equipment, and new innovations.
turns.zh: 我喜欢蒂姆·狄龙。他可能是我最喜欢的，因为他对世界上正在发生的一切——战争、世界新闻和文化——有着最准确、讽刺又 hilarious 的观点。他是我最喜欢的。他昨天刚来过这里。我爱死那家伙了。他太有趣了，太疯狂了。他的思维方式非常独特，而且这得益于他的播客很不一样，他很少请嘉宾。所以大部分时间就是他 rant，他的制作人在笑，他是有史以来最会 rant 的人。我认为没有人能接近他。他是史上最佳。毫无争议。每个喜剧演员都同意，就坐在麦克风前 rant 的能力而言。比如比尔·伯尔做得很好，他很擅长。还有几个家伙也做得不错。但没人比得上蒂姆。他是最持续有趣的。然后为了放松大脑，不是无脑，而是逃避，我听很多射箭节目和狩猎节目，他们谈论不同的狩猎策略、不同的射箭技巧、新设备和新创新。

turns.en: But what the... And you're able to feel those improvements.
turns.zh: 但是……而且你能感受到那些改进。

turns.en: Oh yeah. Yeah. You feel the difference. Every year Hoy puts out a new bow and every year I'm like, "Motherfucker, they did it again. It's better." So I get really fascinated by engineering, really fascinated by automotive engineering. I'm really interested in that's another thing where every year people figure out how to make a car that can hold more G's on a skid pad, that can get around a track quicker. Like every year they're battling to see who can get around the Nürburgring quicker. And what are they doing? They're adding horsepower, increasing suspension travel and suspension tuning, making them more compliant or stiffer and more adjustable, and then tire compounds. I'm just interested in anything where someone's working on something and getting better at it or getting new information. I love history podcasts. I listen to a bunch of history podcasts.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。是的。你能感觉到不同。每年 Hoy 都会推出一款新弓，每年我都会想，‘妈的，他们又做到了。更好了。’所以只是微小的变化，手上的震动更少，射击更平衡，精度更宽容。我喜欢这些东西。所以我对工程学非常着迷，对汽车工程也非常着迷。我真的很感兴趣，这是另一件事，每年人们都在想办法让一辆车在滑行垫上承受更多的 G 力，在赛道上跑得更快。就像每年他们都在争夺谁能在纽博格林赛道上跑得更快。他们在做什么？他们在增加马力，增加悬挂行程和悬挂调校，让它们更顺从或更硬，更可调，还有轮胎配方。我对任何有人正在研究并变得更好或获得新信息的东西都感兴趣。我喜欢历史播客。我听很多历史播客。

turns.zh: 也许。或者也许有别的什么。或者也许他们使用了其他不想让敌人知道的方法。也许这些人身上有某种信标。是的。我想中情局有什么动机去实际描述他们的技术如何工作？

turns.en: Purported surveillance technology utilizes long range quantum magnetometry. What is that? Quantum magnetometers measure extremely faint magnetic fields including the body's natural electromagnetic signals by tracking changes in the energy states of atoms or subatomic particles.
turns.zh: 所谓的监控技术利用远程量子磁力测量。那是什么？量子磁力计通过跟踪原子或亚原子粒子能量状态的变化来测量极其微弱的磁场，包括人体自然的电磁信号。

turns.en: What technology reportedly uses microscopic defects in synthetic diamonds. When illuminated by a laser, these centers are hyper sensitive to tiny magnetic fluctuations. The heart signal, while human heartbeats produce a magnetic field, is extremely weak around 50 to 100 pico teslas and typically degrades over very short distances. So the Ghost Murmur deployment, they reportedly used Ghost Murmur during a mission in southern Iran to pinpoint the location of a downed American airman hiding in dense mountainous terrain by mounting these quantum sensors into a helicopter. The system purportedly registered the pilot's heartbeat from afar. Okay, does that sound like horseshit? I mean, it doesn't sound full of shit, but basically the part that sounds surprising to me is how they're able to deal with all this distance and attenuation across the distance, right? And all this interference and they claim to use AI for that, but nothing is really described on how they use it, right? So, if they're not describing how they use it, why are they even telling us they have it? Why? So, there's a lot of skepticism on it.
turns.zh: 什么技术据说利用了合成钻石中的微观缺陷？当用激光照射时，这些中心对微小的磁波动超敏感。心脏信号，虽然人类心跳会产生磁场，但非常微弱，大约 50 到 100 皮特斯拉，而且通常会在很短的距离内衰减。所以 Ghost Murmur 的部署，据报道他们在伊朗南部的一次任务中使用了 Ghost Murmur，通过将这些量子传感器安装在直升机上，精确定位了一名躲藏在茂密山区中的美国飞行员的位置。该系统据称从远处捕捉到了飞行员的心跳。好吧，这听起来像胡扯吗？我的意思是，听起来不完全是胡扯，但基本上让我惊讶的部分是他们如何应对所有这些距离和距离上的衰减，对吧？以及所有干扰，他们声称用 AI 来处理，但完全没有描述他们如何使用 AI，对吧？所以，如果他们不描述如何使用，为什么还要告诉我们他们有这个？为什么？所以，对此有很多怀疑。

turns.en: Yeah. Laws of physics. Physicists point out that the heart's magnetic field is a million times weaker than the Earth's. Detecting it at a range of miles rather than centimeters defies currently published peer-reviewed physics. Alternative explanations suspect that while quantum sensors were likely on board, they were probably tracking the radio waves of a survival beacon, the metal in the pilot's equipment, or using traditional thermal, infrared, and radar capabilities rather than detecting a raw heartbeat via magnetic fields. I do remember seeing a different part of that story when it happened back in April. Someone did report on one of the military websites that there was a survival beacon that they used to track them, and that the whole quantum mumbo jumbo stuff is like nonsense.
turns.zh: 是啊。物理定律。物理学家指出，心脏的磁场比地球磁场弱一百万倍。在数英里而不是厘米的距离上探测它，违背了目前发表的同行评审物理学。替代解释怀疑，虽然量子传感器可能确实在机上，但他们可能是在追踪生存信标的无线电波、飞行员装备中的金属，或者使用传统的热成像、红外和雷达能力，而不是通过磁场探测原始心跳。我确实记得当那个故事在四月份发生时，我看到了另一部分。有人在某个军事网站上报道说，他们用了一个生存信标来追踪他们，而整个量子胡扯都是胡说八道。

turns.en: Yeah, I saw that too. No one wants to report that because it's not fun, right? No, the Ghost Murmur thing is awesome fun. And if that is real, like boy, you can imagine a world a 100 years from now where that is real. So, it's exciting.
turns.zh: 是啊，我也看到了。没人想报道那个，因为不好玩，对吧？不，Ghost Murmur 的事情超好玩。如果那是真的，天哪，你可以想象一个 100 年后的世界，那是真的。所以，很令人兴奋。

turns.en: Oh, yeah. 100 years is a long time for this to be real.
turns.zh: 哦，是啊。100 年时间很长，足以让这成为现实。

turns.en: Yeah. 100 years they probably got it down pat. Then that's the problem. You can't hide from the robot dogs from Black Mirror.
turns.zh: 是啊。100 年后他们可能已经掌握得很好了。那问题就来了。你躲不过《黑镜》里的机器狗了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

transcript.sec: AI and Human Agency: Doom Moments

turns.en: Do you ever while you're working in AI, do you ever wonder like is this the downfall of humanity? Is this a good thing to be worked on? Did you ever have like doom moments?
turns.zh: 你在从事 AI 工作时，有没有想过这是不是人类的衰落？这是不是一件值得做的好事？你有没有过那种末日时刻？

turns.en: Not on specific things I'm working on, but in general I do worry about how much you obviously want to stay in charge and be in control of your experience, still be the one driving change and have a lot of agency for yourself. So I do worry that it's all about making sure everybody's upskilled and understanding where the future is headed and not being fed only dangerous apocalyptic messages, because it's very essential that human beings retain their agency and staying curious, right? Like if that stops being the case, if you start subscribing to the vision that okay, your jobs are done, you don't really have any meaning in the world and we'll pay you some dividends and you just sit at home and chill, that is not a good thing. So I feel like there are not enough voices in AI that are actually saying anything different to that. And I like when Jensen was here, I think he was a little different. I think he tried to give a more positive version where he said okay, like the radiologist thing, if all radiologists can take away, you know they start doing different kind of work. So I think we need to start looking at okay, first of all guys relax, you have one premium skill: your curiosity. So let's figure out ways to channelize that, let's change the way work is done at companies, let's change the way educational institutions run, let's change the incentive structures, and let's help you build new ideas on new companies and explore things that are not even being considered. And the government should obviously support all these initiatives. So that's what needs to happen more. But what's happening actually right now is okay, hey guys you're all losers. You're going to lose your jobs and don't blame me because I told you so. Right. And still give us money because we're still going to do it anyway. And so that's what's happening more and I think we should stop doing that. That's my opinion.
turns.zh: 不是针对我具体在做的项目，但总的来说，我确实担心，你显然想保持主导地位，控制自己的体验，仍然是推动变革的人，拥有很多自主权。所以我确实担心，关键在于确保每个人都提升技能，理解未来的方向，而不是只被灌输危险的末日信息，因为人类保持自主权和好奇心至关重要，对吧？如果情况不再是这样，如果你开始接受这样的愿景：好吧，你的工作做完了，你在世界上没有任何意义了，我们会给你一些分红，你就坐在家里放松，那可不是好事。所以我觉得 AI 领域没有足够多的声音在说不同的话。我喜欢 Jensen 来的时候，我觉得他有点不同。他试图给出一个更积极的版本，他说，好吧，就像放射科医生的事，如果所有放射科医生都被取代了，你知道他们会开始做不同类型的工作。所以我认为我们需要开始思考：首先，大家放松，你们有一个宝贵的技能：你们的好奇心。所以让我们想办法引导它，改变公司的工作方式，改变教育机构的运行方式，改变激励结构，帮助你们在新公司里建立新想法，探索甚至还没被考虑的事情。政府显然应该支持所有这些举措。所以这才是需要更多发生的。但实际正在发生的是：好吧，嘿，你们都是失败者。你们会失去工作，别怪我，因为我告诉过你们了。对吧。而且还要给我们钱，因为我们无论如何都会做。所以这才是更多发生的，我认为我们应该停止那样做。这是我的观点。

turns.en: Well, it is. The problem is it's kind of a self-fulfilling prophecy. And if you tell people that they're going to be a loser and their life is over, they're going to think that way instead of giving them an understanding of like, look, this can open up new doors for you. This can, and anytime there's any sort of disruptive technology, there's always the fear that it's going to go badly. This was the case with the locomotive. This was the case with when the printing press was invented.
turns.zh: 嗯，确实如此。问题在于这有点像自我实现的预言。如果你告诉人们他们会成为失败者，他们的生活结束了，他们就会那样想，而不是让他们理解：看，这可以为你打开新的大门。这可以，而且每当有颠覆性技术出现时，总是有恐惧它会变糟。火车是这样，印刷术发明时也是这样。

turns.en: Yeah. By the way, I did some research on this where the industrial revolution happened. People got new ideas. For example, when the industrial revolution happened, who came up with the idea of a steel plow? John Deere. Until then we were using wooden plows for farming. No farmer complained that hey, we need fewer farmers now because the steel plow is able to do it more effectively. No one complained. You actually had more farms and more productivity, more crop yields, and you're happier.
turns.zh: 是啊。顺便说一句，我对此做过一些研究，工业革命发生时，人们有了新想法。例如，工业革命发生时，谁想出了钢犁？约翰·迪尔。在那之前我们用木犁耕地。没有农民抱怨说，嘿，我们现在需要更少的农民，因为钢犁能更有效地耕地。没有人抱怨。实际上你有了更多的农场、更高的生产力、更多的作物产量，而且你更快乐了。

turns.en: But isn't that just a regular tool as opposed to AI?
turns.zh: 但这不是普通的工具吗，和 AI 不同？

turns.en: Sure, AI is different. It's not overnight going to become something that's capable of just running an entire multi-trillion dollar company on its own. There are a lot of things that AIs cannot do. There's a lot of tacit knowledge in every company that AIs don't quite understand. And there's a lot of new directions that you can just start working on that AIs are not well equipped to do because it doesn't have full knowledge about it and the knowledge about it is yet to be captured, and some of that requires human-to-human work and collaboration. So we obviously have to gravitate towards what is scarce. When AI makes the current labor that's considered scarce because that's where the money is going in commodity, then we have to gravitate towards what is scarce, and the only way to do that is to seek things that we don't know about, which is only something we can discover through our curiosity. There's nothing else.
turns.zh: 当然，AI 是不同的。它不会一夜之间变成能够独立运营整个数万亿美元公司的东西。有很多事情 AI 做不到。每个公司都有很多隐性知识，AI 并不完全理解。而且有很多新方向你可以开始研究，AI 并不擅长做这些，因为它没有关于它的完整知识，而关于它的知识还有待捕捉，其中一些需要人与人之间的工作和协作。所以我们显然必须转向稀缺的东西。当 AI 使当前被认为是稀缺的劳动变成商品时，因为钱都流向那里，那么我们就必须转向稀缺的东西，而做到这一点的唯一方法就是寻求我们不知道的东西，这只能通过我们的好奇心去发现。没有别的办法。

transcript.secZh: AI 与人类能动性：末日时刻

transcript.sec: Redefining Work and Passion in the AI Era

turns.en: Whatever we don't quite understand well, whatever we don't know how to do well yet, even with the current capabilities of AI, that's where we should pull our labor and workforce into. So, it needs more responsible messaging and that's not quite happening right now. I think it needs responsible messaging and then in the future what it needs is like real direction in terms of like letting people find their curiosity and find these paths of interest and find something to do with themselves that doesn't involve whatever their previous occupation that's irrelevant now.
turns.zh: 凡是那些我们还不完全理解、还不太会做的事情，即使以当前 AI 的能力，也应该把劳动力和人力投入进去。所以，这需要更负责任的宣传，而目前并没有做到。我认为需要负责任的宣传，未来还需要真正的引导，让人们找到自己的好奇心，找到感兴趣的路径，找到与自己相关的事情去做，而不是他们之前那些已经无关紧要的职业。

turns.en: Mhm. That's true. I think like passion for people is something that not a lot of people would be able to answer out of the box. Like if you go and ask them what is your real passion and the only thing they have known in life is to just climb up career ladders and make more money. That's going to actually take them a while to even discover.
turns.zh: 嗯，确实如此。我觉得对很多人来说，激情并不是能脱口而出的东西。如果你去问他们真正的激情是什么，而他们一生只知道爬职业阶梯、赚更多钱，那他们甚至需要一段时间才能发现。

turns.en: And which is why it's so important to get kids off on the right start.
turns.zh: 所以让孩子从一开始就走上正确的道路非常重要。

turns.en: Yeah. That's the hope. That's our hope for the future is the kids are born curious. They don't need to change themselves to be curious, right?
turns.zh: 是的，这就是希望。我们对未来的希望是，孩子们天生好奇。他们不需要改变自己去变得好奇，对吧？

turns.en: The adults who probably already are like because of this knowledge work thing who kind of curb their curiosity and try to fit into the existing system, it might be a little hard for them to adapt. But the kids, I think they don't have this problem. So I'm actually optimistic about the future long term because the future is all centered around like whoever is like very young today.
turns.zh: 那些成年人，可能因为知识工作这件事，已经抑制了好奇心，试图适应现有体系，他们适应起来可能会有点困难。但孩子们，我认为他们没有这个问题。所以我对长期未来其实很乐观，因为未来完全围绕着今天还很年轻的人。

turns.en: What do you think about this idea that universal basic income is going to be required?
turns.zh: 你怎么看普遍基本收入是必要的这个想法？

turns.en: Some form of it is good. Some it's like a dividend. I almost think of it as a dividend. If a lot of spend that most companies are currently doing today on like payroll, which is paying a knowledge worker for a certain task. Think of knowledge work is basically taking information and transforming it into an artifact, right? And it's messy and complicated. Let's assume that's being done by AIs. So obviously companies will start spending more on compute instead of payroll. It's just a reallocation of like spend or budget similar to like what happened in advertising industries where most of your advertising budgets went to like television and like billboards and then now it's starting to go to Google and Instagram and YouTube and all that. So when that happens obviously like the AI companies are going to make a lot of money and people who helped be part of creating it or like either directly or indirectly would want to have some role to play in that ecosystem and a good way to involve them is through giving them some ownership in the company. So as shareholders you if you get dividends from the profits generated by the AIs it's not a bad thing but that shouldn't be the only thing.
turns.zh: 某种形式的基本收入是好的。有点像分红。我几乎把它看作分红。如果大多数公司目前花在工资上的大量支出，也就是支付知识工作者完成特定任务的费用——知识工作基本上是把信息转化为人工制品，对吧？它又乱又复杂。假设这些由 AI 完成，那么显然公司会开始把更多支出花在算力上，而不是工资上。这只是支出或预算的重新分配，类似于广告行业发生的情况：过去大部分广告预算花在电视和广告牌上，现在开始流向 Google、Instagram、YouTube 等等。所以当这种情况发生时，显然 AI 公司会赚很多钱，而那些直接或间接参与创造 AI 的人，会希望在这个生态系统中扮演某种角色。让他们参与进来的一个好方法是给予他们公司的一些所有权。作为股东，如果你从 AI 产生的利润中获得分红，这不是坏事，但这不应该是唯一的方式。

turns.en: Right, so this is similar to like people that live in Alaska they get a check because Alaska does this and it's not a bad thing as long as they are doing some other things alongside.
turns.zh: 对，这类似于阿拉斯加居民会收到一张支票，因为阿拉斯加就是这么做的，只要他们同时在做其他事情，这也不是坏事。

turns.en: Correct. It could lessen the burden.
turns.zh: 没错。这可以减轻负担。

turns.en: Yeah, and if people are interested in still being part of the AI industries they go do things that AIs are not able to do today. And that's been the case before like when industrial revolution started the United Kingdom actually started like projects around building railroads and that gave a lot of people who are in the cottage industries new jobs. So there are going to be a lot of new projects to just okay like what if we want to reimagine the government itself where the government runs largely on AI.
turns.zh: 是的，如果人们仍然有兴趣参与 AI 行业，他们可以去做 AI 目前做不到的事情。以前也是这样，比如工业革命开始时，英国实际上启动了修建铁路等项目，这给许多从事家庭手工业的人提供了新工作。所以未来会有很多新项目，比如如果我们想重新构想政府本身，让政府主要靠 AI 运行。

turns.en: Yeah, that was my next question.
turns.zh: 是的，这正是我下一个问题。

turns.en: Yeah. So then we need people for that.
turns.zh: 是的，所以我们需要人来干这个。

turns.en: Because this is a legacy industry. It's not about the capabilities not being there. It's about working through the legacy and bureaucracy to like actually deploy and implement this inside the most like largest institutions in the country and that's going to need a new set of skilled workers to go do that. So some people who might be working at Microsoft or something today might actually end up working for the United States government because Microsoft may not need them especially for like you know internally deploying AI or selling AI to their customers but the government needs them and if the government can pay them well and it's a fulfilling job to find some meaning for like doing something good for the country it's not a bad thing. So I think like just like in the industrial revolution where we had new projects because the demand for AI was so big, we're going to start seeing some new projects being created in AI as well when the capabilities advance enough that they can replace knowledge workers.
turns.zh: 因为这是一个遗留行业。不是能力不够，而是要克服遗留系统和官僚主义，真正在美国最大的机构内部部署和实施这些技术，这需要一批新的技能工人去做。所以，一些今天可能在微软工作的人，最终可能会为美国政府工作，因为微软可能不再需要他们，尤其是在内部部署 AI 或向客户销售 AI 方面，但政府需要他们。如果政府能给他们好的报酬，而且这是一份有成就感的工作，能为国家做点好事，那也不是坏事。所以我认为，就像工业革命时期，因为对 AI 的需求巨大，我们会有新项目一样，当 AI 能力足够进步到可以取代知识工作者时，我们也会开始看到 AI 领域出现一些新项目。

turns.en: That's the rosy scenario.
turns.zh: 这是乐观的情景。

turns.en: It's not as rosy like real world is messy. A lot of things are still done through trusting other human beings. Nobody's like blindly trusting AIs. AI still make a lot of mistakes. I know a lot of people are hesitant to the idea of AI running government and I get it. But also look at what the people are doing. Look at how much corruption there is, how much fraud and waste. Imagine if all fraud, waste, and corruption was instantaneously eliminated.
turns.zh: 并没有那么乐观，现实世界是混乱的。很多事情仍然依赖于信任他人。没有人会盲目信任 AI。AI 仍然会犯很多错误。我知道很多人对 AI 管理政府的想法犹豫不决，我理解。但也要看看人们在做什么。看看有多少腐败、欺诈和浪费。想象一下，如果所有欺诈、浪费和腐败瞬间被消除。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: I mean, that was what Elon tried to do with Doge, right?
turns.zh: 我的意思是，这就是埃隆试图用 Doge 做的事情，对吧？

turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: And then I think the bottleneck there was just discovering how slow it is to do things. It's not he's not used to running that slow.
turns.zh: 然后我认为瓶颈在于发现做事有多慢。他不习惯那么慢的节奏。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. And also how much resistance because there was so much grift.
turns.zh: 是啊，还有多大的阻力，因为里面有那么多骗局。

So honestly like more than AI the government is running a lot of legacy software stack because a lot of these legacy enterprise companies just have created these multi-decade or like year contracts that are hard to get out of.
